text: The 68th Siege Battery, Royal Garrison Artillery (RGA), was a British Army unit active from October 9, 1915, until 1919. Formed from the Pembroke Royal Garrison Artillery, it served in key engagements during World War I, including the Battles of the Somme and Passchendaele. The battery was involved in significant artillery operations, providing support during various offensives and was ultimately disbanded in 1919.
Elena Vallortigara (born September 21, 1991) is an Italian high jumper. She won bronze medals at the youth world level and eight national championships.

Mesa/Boogie is an American company that designs and manufactures amplifiers for guitars and basses. Founded in 1969 by Randall Smith in Petaluma, California, the company gained recognition for its innovative amplifier designs, including the high-gain Mark I and the Rectifier series, which became staples in modern rock music. Originally starting as a small repair shop, Mesa/Boogie evolved into a prominent amplifier manufacturer, with Smith pioneering advancements such as cascading gain stages. The company relocated to Petaluma in 1980 and continued to expand its product line over the decades. In 2021, Mesa/Boogie was acquired by Gibson, with Smith joining Gibson as a "Master Designer." The company remains a significant name in the music industry.
Beverly Williams is an American news anchor and reporter, known for her work at KYW-TV.

The Christmas in Spiceworld Tour was a 1999 concert tour by the Spice Girls, featuring eight shows in the UK. It included elaborate staging, a mix of their hits and new songs, and was broadcast live on Sky One. A crew member died in an accident after the final show.

The Forward Gal Stakes is a Grade III American Thoroughbred horse race held annually at Gulfstream Park in Hallandale, Florida. Inaugurated in 1981, the seven-furlong dirt race is open to three-year-old fillies and offers a $100,000 purse. It serves as a qualifying event for the Kentucky Oaks.

The General der Nachrichtenaufklärung Training Referat was a German military signals intelligence training unit during World War II. It provided specialized instruction in cryptanalysis, radio monitoring, and cipher techniques to personnel in the Wehrmacht's signal intelligence divisions. Courses included cryptanalysis of substitution and transposition ciphers, machine ciphers, and language-specific training for intercept operators. The program aimed to address shortages in skilled cryptanalysts and improve field intelligence operations, though effectiveness declined later in the war due to organizational disruptions.

Sergio Llull (born November 15, 1987) is a Spanish professional basketball player for Real Madrid of the Liga ACB and the EuroLeague. A versatile guard, he has spent his entire professional career with Real Madrid since 2007, winning two EuroLeague titles (2015, 2018) and seven Liga ACB championships. Llull was named EuroLeague MVP in 2017 and has earned multiple individual honors, including Liga ACB MVP and Finals MVP. Drafted by the Denver Nuggets in 2009, his NBA rights are currently held by the New York Knicks. With the Spanish national team, he has won gold at the 2019 FIBA World Cup and three EuroBasket titles (2009, 2011, 2015), along with Olympic silver (2012) and bronze (2016).

*Lunella undulata* is a marine snail species endemic to Australia, found in coastal waters from New South Wales to Western Australia. It is notable for its bright green, umbilicate shell and presence in archaeological middens.
Newman O'Neal was the mayor of Hobson City, Alabama, in 1919, forced into exile after receiving death threats during the Red Summer.

*Clepsicosma* is a genus of moths in the family Crambidae, containing the single described species *Clepsicosma iridia*. Both the genus and species were first described by Edward Meyrick in 1888, based on a female specimen collected in the Waitākere Ranges, Auckland, New Zealand. The species is endemic to New Zealand, where it is commonly found in native forests across the North Island and parts of the South Island. Adult moths are nocturnal, active from late December to May, and are attracted to light. They rest on the underside of leaves during the day. The larval host plant is believed to be species of cutty grass, particularly *Gahnia setifolia* and *Gahnia xanthocarpa*, though this has not been confirmed through rearing in captivity.

Andy González (January 1, 1951 – April 9, 2020) was a prominent American musician known for his contributions to Afro-Cuban jazz, salsa, son cubano, boogaloo, and pachanga. Born in New York City, González co-founded Conjunto Libre in 1974 with his brother Jerry, producing influential albums such as "Concepts in Unity" (1975) and "Lo Dice Todo" (1976). He also led The Fort Apache band and collaborated with notable artists like Tito Puente, Eddie Palmieri, and Ray Barreto. González's unique ability to play creatively within the tumbao patterns earned him widespread recognition. In 2016, he released his debut album "Entre Colegas," which was nominated for a Grammy Award. González passed away from pneumonia and diabetes complications in the Bronx in 2020.

Red Obsession, a 2013 Australian documentary directed and co-written by David Roach and Warwick Ross, explores China's wine boom. Praised for its cinematography and insightful analysis, the film received a 100% approval rating from critics.

The Maquis des Glières was a French Resistance group active during World War II, notably involved in the Battle of Glières from January to March 1944. Despite being besieged and ultimately defeated by German and Vichy forces, the battle served as a significant propaganda boost for the Resistance.
Maria Clara Eimmart was a German astronomer from Nuremberg, known for her precise astronomical illustrations, particularly of the moon's phases, and for her contributions to her father's observatory.

John Landsteiner (born May 19, 1990) is an American curler and Olympic gold medalist. Playing lead for Team John Shuster, he has represented the United States in three Winter Olympics (2014, 2018, 2022), winning gold at the 2018 PyeongChang Games. Landsteiner has also competed in five World Curling Championships and multiple U.S. National Championships, earning several titles. A civil engineer by profession, he resides in Duluth, Minnesota, and works as an integrity engineer.

The *Oberkommando der Luftwaffe* (OKL) was the High Command of Nazi Germany's Luftwaffe, established in 1935 to oversee military aviation operations. It reported to the *Oberkommando der Wehrmacht* (OKW) and played a key role in organizing and commanding air fleets, air districts, and operational units during World War II. Initially part of Hermann Göring's Ministry of Aviation, the OKL evolved into a distinct military command structure, though its organization remained fragmented. It was disbanded in 1945 following Germany's defeat.

"Road Rules: Down Under" is the sixth season of the reality television series "Road Rules," which aired on MTV from June 22 to September 7, 1998. The season featured a cast including Chadwick Pelletier, Christina Pazsitzky, and Susie Meister, and consisted of 13 episodes.

The Right (Italian: *La Destra*) was a far-right political party in Italy, founded in 2007 by Francesco Storace after splitting from the National Alliance. The party promoted neofascism, national conservatism, Euroscepticism, and a mix of statist and libertarian economic policies. It contested elections independently and in alliances but failed to gain significant electoral success, dissolving in 2017 to merge into the National Movement for Sovereignty.

A marine evacuation system (MES) is a life-saving appliance (LSA) designed for the rapid mass evacuation of passengers and crew from a ship or offshore installation. It is capable of evacuating over 700 people in 30 minutes. MES has gained popularity in the maritime industry over the past 30 years due to its efficiency and safety-enhancing capabilities. It has been tested in harsh marine conditions, including Beaufort Sea State 6 winds and significant wave heights.

Deployment of an MES requires minimal staff, typically one or two people. The system automatically deploys a chute into the water, where a life raft inflates. The entire process takes approximately 90 seconds, with some systems inflating within one minute. Evacuees then slide down the air-cushioned chutes into the life rafts. The MES consists of 5 components.
Egypt competed at the 2014 Summer Youth Olympics in Nanjing, China, with 83 athletes in 20 sports, winning eight medals.

An extraocular implant, also known as eyeball jewelry, is a form of body modification developed in the Netherlands in 2002. The procedure involves implanting small platinum alloy shapes into the eye's sclera.

Modesto Marini is an Italian chef and restaurateur, best known as the founder of The Marini's Group, which includes high-end establishments such as Marini's on 57, Marble 8, and M Marini Caffè in Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia. Born near Lake Garda, Italy, he trained professionally before working at Cecconi's in London and later moving to Southeast Asia. After launching the successful Modesto's restaurant chain, he sold his shares and established Marini's on 57 in 2012—Malaysia’s highest rooftop bar and restaurant—followed by other luxury dining venues. His establishments have hosted celebrities, athletes, and royalty, including Formula One drivers and musicians. Marini has also collaborated with renowned DJs to produce the *Sunset Hours* chill-out music series. Recognized for his contributions to hospitality, he has received awards such as the Hospitality Asia Platinum Icon of the Year and the Superbrands award. He resides in Malaysia with his wife and two children.
South Venice is a census-designated place (CDP) in Sarasota County, Florida, United States. The population was 15,619 at the 2020 census.

Joseph Holston, raised in Hawkins Lane, Chevy Chase, Maryland, is a renowned artist whose work reflects strong Black identity. After a career as a commercial artist, he shifted to full-time painting and etching, influenced by Rembrandt. His artwork, including the "Color in Freedom" series, explores the journey from slavery to freedom. Holston has exhibited widely and received recognition for his contributions to art and culture.

Janet Haworth is a Welsh politician who served as a Conservative Member of the Welsh Assembly for the North Wales region from May 2015 to April 2016. She succeeded Antoinette Sandbach following the latter's election to the UK Parliament. Previously a Liberal Democrat, Haworth joined the Conservative Party in 2004 and resides in Llandudno.

William Henry Seaman (1842–1915) was a United States Circuit Judge of the Seventh Circuit, a United States District Judge for the Eastern District of Wisconsin, and the 22nd Mayor of Sheboygan, Wisconsin. He also served in the Union Army during the American Civil War.

StarUML is a proprietary Unified Modeling Language (UML) tool developed by MKLabs, initially released in 2006. It evolved from the earlier Plastic software and supports UML 2.0, SysML, and legacy modeling notations. Originally open-source, StarUML transitioned to a commercial model in 2014, with the latest version, 5.0, released in 2022. It is compatible with Windows, macOS, and Linux, offering features like diagram generation, model documentation, and file format support for JSON, HTML, and image exports. StarUML is widely used in academia and software modeling, ranking as the third most-used tool in a 2017 study.
Lugazi University was a private institution in Uganda, active from 2007 to 2010, before closing due to financial difficulties.

"Faces of Ground Zero" is a photographic exhibition that features portraits of individuals who were affected by the September 11 attacks in 2001. The exhibit was displayed at various prominent locations throughout 2002, including Vanderbilt Hall at Grand Central Terminal in New York City, the Boston Public Library, and the Skirball Cultural Center in Los Angeles. It returned to New York for the first anniversary of 9/11, showcasing at Rockefeller Center, and was later featured again on the fifth anniversary at the New York City Fire Museum.

To commemorate the tenth anniversary of the attacks, a special exhibition titled "Faces of Ground Zero: 10 Years Later" was held at the Time Warner Center, presenting over 50 images from the original collection along with new photographs. The exhibition aimed to honor the memories of those impacted by the tragedy and to provide a visual narrative of resilience and remembrance. Additionally, many portraits from the exhibit were published in photobooks, further extending the reach and impact of the project.

Jeremy Maurice Jacobs (born January 21, 1940) is an American billionaire businessman, chairman of Delaware North, and owner of the Boston Bruins of the National Hockey League (NHL) since 1975. He has served as chairman of the NHL Board of Governors since 2007. Jacobs is known for his significant contributions to sports, philanthropy, and business, including overseeing Delaware North's global operations in hospitality and food services. Under his ownership, the Bruins won the Stanley Cup in 2011. A major philanthropist, Jacobs and his family have donated extensively to institutions such as the University at Buffalo, where the medical school is named in their honor.

Nestor Principe (March 15, 1945 – May 7, 1973) was a Filipino activist, martial arts instructor, and writer. A black belt in Sikaran, he introduced the martial art to Malaysia before traveling extensively and later returning to the Philippines. Enrolling at the Lyceum of the Philippines, he became a student activist and published literary works. He was killed by Philippine Constabulary forces in 1973, with his decapitation becoming a notorious act of brutality. In 2018, he was honored at the Bantayog ng mga Bayani for his resistance against the Marcos dictatorship.

The National Audit Office (NAO) of Tanzania, formed in 2001, succeeded the Exchequer and Audit Department. It operates under the Public Finance Act, No. 6 of 2001 and is headquartered in Dodoma. The NAO oversees public finances and is led by Charles Kichere, the Auditor General. Previously known as the Exchequer and Audit Department, it evolved from the Overseas Audit Services under British colonial rule.

Give Notice was a British-bred bay gelding foaled in 1997. Trained by John Dunlop, he was owned by Ian Stewart-Brown and Michael Meacock. Give Notice is notable for winning the Stubbs Stakes in 2001, the Sagaro Stakes in 2002, and the Prix du Cadran in 2002. He was sired by Warning and his dam was Princess Genista.

Moses Rischin (born in Brooklyn, New York) was an American historian and professor at San Francisco State University. He earned his Ph.D. from Harvard University in 1957 and later directed the Western Jewish History Center at the Judah L. Magnes Museum. Rischin served on the board of the *Journal of American Ethnic History* and the American Jewish History Society. In 1996, a collection of essays was published in his honor. A character in Helen Hudson’s 1967 novel *Meyer Meyer* may have been inspired by him.
FC Ingolstadt 04 II is the reserve team of FC Ingolstadt 04, founded in 2004. The team competes in the Bayernliga Süd and has previously played in the Regionalliga Bayern.

The 1965 Pakistani presidential election was held on 2 January 1965, in which incumbent President Ayub Khan defeated Fatima Jinnah, the sister of Pakistan's founder, Muhammad Ali Jinnah. The election was conducted under the Basic Democracy system, where 80,000 Basic Democrats served as an electoral college.  

Ayub Khan, who had ruled since the 1958 military coup, campaigned on his economic reforms and political stability. Fatima Jinnah, backed by the Combined Opposition Parties (COP), challenged his authoritarian rule but faced restrictions, including limited campaigning and clerical opposition to a female leader. Despite strong urban support, particularly in Karachi and Dhaka, Jinnah lost due to Ayub's rural dominance.  

Ayub won 62.43% of the electoral vote, while Jinnah secured 35.86%. The opposition alleged rigging, but protests faded after Jinnah accepted the results. The election marked a significant moment for women in Pakistani politics, encouraging greater female participation in later years.

*Aadmi* (1993) is a Hindi action drama film directed by Arshad Khan, starring Mithun Chakraborty as Vijay, an honest man framed for murder who seeks justice against corrupt officials. The film features music by Jatin–Lalit and includes a rare positive role by Shakti Kapoor.

Mary McIvor (born Mary Ellsworth McKeever; August 31, 1897 – February 28, 1941) was an American actress known for her roles in Westerns. She was the leading lady for William S. Hart and later married actor William Desmond in 1919, retiring from acting shortly after. McIvor faced significant health challenges later in life, including amnesia, and died of a heart attack in 1941.

The Commissioner of the Delhi Police is the head of the Delhi Police, appointed by the Ministry of Home Affairs. Established in 1978 under the Delhi Police Act, the commissioner holds the rank of Director General of Police. The current commissioner is Sanjay Arora, IPS, who assumed office on 1 August 2022.

Infection and Immunity is a monthly peer-reviewed scientific journal focusing on infectious disease and immunity. Established in 1970, it publishes original research articles previously featured in a section of the Journal of Bacteriology. Edited by Andreas J. Bäumler, the journal has an impact factor of 3.256 (2017), ranking 30th in "Infectious Diseases" and 75th in "Immunology." It is published by the American Society for Microbiology and has been recognized for its contributions to the field through abstracting and indexing services.

Altibbi is an Arabic-language digital health platform founded in 2008 in Amman, Jordan, by Jalil Labadi. It provides medical information, teleconsultations, and healthcare services across the Middle East and North Africa (MENA) region. The platform, which evolved from an earlier medical glossary by Dr. Abdel Aziz Labadi, connects users with over 12,000 accredited doctors and receives millions of monthly visits. Altibbi secured investments from Middle East Venture Partners and other firms, enabling expansion into multiple Arab countries. Recognized for its contributions to Arabic medical content, it won the Arab E-Content Award in 2013.

The Select Registry, founded in 1972 by travel writer Norman Simpson, identifies and documents high-quality lodging and dining establishments across North America. Headquartered in Greensboro, Georgia, it includes bed and breakfasts, urban inns, boutique hotels, and small resorts, requiring annual dues and periodic quality inspections. The organization celebrates its 50th anniversary in 2022 and publishes the annual guidebook and the bi-annual travel magazine, Inn Scene.
Deitingen is a municipality in the Wasseramt district of the canton of Solothurn, Switzerland, with a population of 2,227 (2020).

"In Melbourne Tonight" was an Australian variety television show hosted by Graham Kennedy, airing from 1957 to 1970 and briefly revived from 1996 to 1998. The program featured a mix of comedy, music, and interviews, becoming a significant part of Australian television history. It showcased numerous presenters and established a legacy of live variety programming, with Kennedy's charisma being a key factor in its success.

Mary Elizabeth Wood (August 22, 1861 – May 1, 1931) was an American librarian and missionary known for pioneering modern library science in China. After moving to Wuchang in 1899, she established the Boone School Library and later founded China's first library school in 1920. She advocated for public libraries, trained Chinese librarians, and secured U.S. funding for library development. Wood remained in China until her death, leaving a lasting impact on Chinese librarianship.

"I Am Real" is the debut and only single by Simon van Kempen, an Australian hotelier and husband of Alex McCord, a cast member of *The Real Housewives of New York City*. Released on April 21, 2011, the dance-pop and EDM track was written by Allison Beth Simon, Jon Jason Appleton, and Koki Saito, with production by Joel Simon, Ross Hopman, and Saito. The song, recorded at JSM Music Studios in New York City, reflects van Kempen's experiences with reality television, addressing criticism, class, and his interactions with cast members of *The Real Housewives*. Its lyrics include playful and self-deprecating commentary, such as the now-famous line "Thug in a cocktail dress," referencing a remark by McCord on the show.

Critics offered mixed to negative reviews, with some calling it a "musical punch line" and others appreciating its uniqueness among *Real Housewives* singles. Despite its notoriety, the song sold approximately 1,000 digital copies in the U.S. and was ranked among the franchise's least successful musical releases. Van Kempen promoted the track with live performances, including on *Watch What Happens Live* and at Manhattan's Fashion Night Out. The song's development and reception were later revisited in Bravo's 2017 web series *Throwback Bravo*.

A mobile is a type of kinetic sculpture characterized by its ability to move, typically through air currents or direct interaction. The term "mobile" was first coined by Marcel Duchamp in 1931 to describe the early mechanized works of Alexander Calder, though it later became more specifically associated with Calder's free-moving, hanging sculptures. Calder's mobiles consist of brightly colored, abstract shapes made from sheet metal, connected by wire in a balanced arrangement. These elements can move independently or as a whole, creating dynamic, ever-changing compositions. Calder is credited with inventing this art form and produced numerous mobiles from the 1930s until his death in 1976, solidifying the term's association with his work.

While Calder's mobiles are the most iconic, similar concepts were explored by other artists, including Man Ray in the 1920s, Armando Reverón in the 1930s, and Bruno Munari, who created his "Useless Machines" in 1933 using playful colors and cardboard.

Scott L. Thoele is an officer in the United States Army, commissioned in 1980. He served in the Gulf War, the Iraq War, and the War in Afghanistan. His awards include the Legion of Merit and the Bronze Star Medal with oak leaf cluster.

As a civilian, Thoele is vice president and credit analyst of First Bankers Trust Company in Quincy, Illinois. He received his education at Quincy University and the University of Wisconsin-Madison.

Protein–protein interaction (PPI) prediction uses computational methods to identify which proteins are likely to interact, based on genomic, evolutionary, or structural data. These methods include phylogenetic profiling, which identifies functionally related proteins based on their co-occurrence across genomes; the Rosetta stone method, which predicts interactions based on gene fusion events in other organisms; and the conserved gene neighborhood method, which identifies functionally related proteins based on their proximity on a chromosome. Other methods include classification methods, inference from homologous structures, association methods, identification of structural patterns, Bayesian network modeling, domain-pair exclusion analysis, and framing the problem as a supervised learning problem. PPI prediction is related to protein–protein docking, which models the 3D structure of protein complexes.

An avulsion injury is the separation of tissue from its attachment point. Skin avulsions range in severity and may require grafts or reconstructive surgery. In rock climbing, skin avulsions called "flappers" can occur on the fingers. Avulsions can also affect the ear, eyelid, nail, brachial plexus, and teeth. Treatment varies depending on the location and severity of the injury, and may include suturing, microvascular surgery, nerve grafts, or tooth re-implantation. In some cases, avulsion is performed surgically to treat certain conditions.

ʿAbd al-Malik ibn ʿUmar ibn Marwān (c. 718 – c. 778) was an Umayyad prince and military commander who played a key role in consolidating the rule of Emir ʿAbd al-Raḥmān I in al-Andalus. A grandson of Caliph Marwān I, he fled to Iberia after the Abbasid Revolution (750) and became a trusted general of the emir. As governor of Seville, he suppressed revolts by local Arab factions, securing Umayyad control over western al-Andalus. His descendants held prominent positions in the Umayyad Emirate of Córdoba, including governorships and military commands. His decisive victories helped establish the Umayyad dynasty's long-term rule in the region.

The Man Who Cheated Himself is a 1950 film noir directed by Felix E. Feist, starring Lee J. Cobb and Jane Wyatt. Set in San Francisco, it follows homicide detective Lt. Ed Cullen as he investigates a murder case involving his ex-lover, leading to a tense confrontation with his brother.

The White Obelisk is a limestone monument from ancient Assyria, dating to approximately 1050–1031 BC. Discovered in Nineveh, Iraq, by archaeologist Hormuzd Rassam in 1853, it was transported to London and is now housed in the British Museum. Standing 284 cm tall, the obelisk features relief carvings and inscriptions depicting military campaigns, royal hunts, banquets, and religious rituals, including a libation to the goddess Ishtar. Believed to date to the reign of Ashurnasirpal I, it is one of the earliest examples of Assyrian art, showcasing intricate details of the period's cultural and political life.

The 0-8-0 is a steam locomotive with eight driving wheels and no leading or trailing wheels. Examples were constructed as both tender and tank locomotives. The wheel arrangement maximized tractive effort, making it suitable for heavy freight and switching duties.
Todd Scott is a New Zealand businessman, former radio host, and television presenter, best known as the owner of *National Business Review*.

The DR Class 52.80 was a series of reconstructed steam locomotives derived from the wartime DRB Class 52, rebuilt between 1960 and 1967 at the Stendal Reichsbahn repair shop. Featuring upgraded boilers, welded cylinders, and other modifications, 200 locomotives were modernized for improved performance. Several examples survive today, including the extensively rebuilt 52 8055, certified for operation in Switzerland.
Design Museum Den Bosch is a museum in 's-Hertogenbosch, Netherlands, focusing on contemporary visual arts and design, especially ceramic art and jewelry.

The mountain ground squirrel (Geosciurus princeps) is a rodent found in the southwest arid region of Africa, from southern Angola to southern Namibia and as far south as Richtersveld National Park. It is classified as Least Concern on the IUCN Red List.

This large-bodied squirrel has small ears, with a head and body length of 23 to 29 cm, a tail length of 21 to 28 cm, and a weight of 490 to 710 grams. Its body is covered in short, pale cinnamon brown hair, with white fur on its belly, around the eyes, and on the front of its face. A white stripe extends from its shoulders to its hips. Mountain ground squirrels are diurnal, with females living alone or in small family groups, while males are mostly solitary.

Gayopakhyanam is a Telugu play written by Chilakamarti Lakshmi Narasimham in 1890, based on the story of Gaya. Premiered in April 1890, it features characters like Gaya, Arjuna, and Sri Krishna. The play, published in 1909, sold over a lakh copies. It depicts Gaya's conflict with Sri Krishna, resolved by Lord Shiva, and tests Arjuna's commitment. Adapted into films in Telugu and Tamil as Sri Krishnarjuna Yudham in 1962/1963.

Beenie Gunter, born Crescent Baguma on February 23, 1993, in Kyenjojo, Uganda, is a prominent reggae and dancehall musician. Active since 2013, he gained recognition with singles like "No Offence," "Pon Mi," and "Olina Work." Gunter has collaborated with various artists and won multiple awards, including the Buzz Teenz Awards for Dancehall Artist of the Year. He released his debut album, "No Fear," in August 2019.

Nettle is a low-level cryptographic library written in C, developed by Niels Möller. It provides implementations of symmetric ciphers, hash functions, public-key algorithms, and message authentication codes. Licensed under LGPLv3 and GPL, it is designed for simplicity and efficiency, often used by projects like GnuTLS.

Paulus Vallius, born in Rome, taught at the Collegio Romano in the 1580s and at Padua. His notes influenced Galileo, and he published "Logica" in 1622, siding with Pereyra against Biancani on mathematical proof in physics.

KNBN is a television station based in Rapid City, South Dakota, broadcasting on digital channel 21 and affiliated with NBC and MyNetworkTV. Founded on July 15, 1996, it officially began broadcasting on May 14, 2000. Initially launched as a low-power station, KNBN developed a local news presence with the debut of its NewsCenter1 program in 1997. The station currently produces 16 hours of local newscasts each week and transitioned to a digital signal on February 1, 2009.

Roy Oxlade (1929–2014) was an English painter born in Tottenham, England. Educated at Bromley School of Art, Goldsmiths, and the Royal College of Art, Oxlade is known for his imaginative and metaphorical paintings.
*Cinéaste* is a quarterly American film magazine established in 1967.

*A Walk in the Sun* is a 1945 American war film directed by Lewis Milestone and based on Harry Brown's 1944 novel of the same name. The film follows a platoon of American soldiers during the Allied invasion of Italy in World War II, focusing on their mission to destroy a bridge near a farmhouse while grappling with the psychological and physical toll of combat. Starring Dana Andrews, Richard Conte, and John Ireland, the film is notable for its realistic portrayal of soldiers and its use of ballads by Millard Lampell and Earl Robinson to narrate the story. It was critically acclaimed upon release.

The 1952 Hyderabad State Legislative Assembly election was the first election held in Hyderabad State after its integration into India. The Indian National Congress won a majority, securing 93 of 175 seats with 41.86% of the vote. The People's Democratic Front, representing communists, won all seats in Nalgonda district. Following the States Reorganisation Act of 1956, Hyderabad State was merged into Andhra Pradesh.

Mairéad Uí Choigligh (born in Wexford, Ireland), commonly known as Gretta Kehoe-Quigley, is an Irish camogie player. She debuted with the Wexford junior team in 1969 and won a Leinster Senior Championship medal in 1974.

The Right (Italian: *La Destra*) was a far-right political party in Italy, founded in 2007 by Francesco Storace after splitting from National Alliance. It merged into the National Movement for Sovereignty in 2017.

Alba Vergés i Bosch (born 3 September 1978) is a Spanish economist and politician serving as Acting President of the Parliament of Catalonia since 2022. A member of the Republican Left of Catalonia (ERC), she previously served as Minister of Health (2018–2021) and First Vice President of the Parliament (2021–2022).

County Route 8 (CR 8) is a 5.00-mile (8.05 km) county road in Nassau County, New York. It consists of two segments: Herricks Road, running north from Garden City to Herricks, and Shelter Rock Road, continuing northwest to Manhasset. The route passes through Mineola, North Hills, and other communities, intersecting major roads like NY 25 and NY 25A. A former grade crossing on Herricks Road was the site of a deadly 1982 train crash, leading to the construction of an underpass in 1998.

2011 MD, discovered by LINEAR on June 22, 2011, is a small asteroid categorized as an Apollo and Amor class near-Earth object. Estimated to be about 6 meters in diameter, it made a close approach to Earth in 2011, increasing its orbital period. Observed by the Spitzer Space Telescope in 2014, it was deemed a potential candidate for NASA's Asteroid Redirect Mission.

The Schöneberger Sängerknaben, founded by Gerhard Hellwig in 1947, is a renowned boys' choir known for its extensive performances in operas, including the Bayreuth Festival, and its television appearances. The choir undertook over 300 concert tours globally. Founded in 1947, the choir flourished under Hellwig's leadership until his death in 2011, after which the choir ceased operations.

Kogiidae is a family of small cetaceans, commonly known as kogiids, that includes the dwarf sperm whale (Kogia sima) and the pygmy sperm whale (Kogia breviceps). These whales are characterized by their short rostrums, high levels of asymmetry, and unique sound production organs. Kogiidae are believed to inhabit all oceans except polar regions and primarily feed on squid using suction feeding techniques. Their populations face threats from fishing activities and plastic pollution.

Teresa Dent (born 1959) is a British conservationist and CEO of the Game & Wildlife Conservation Trust since 2001. A former farming consultant, she was appointed CBE in 2015 for services to wildlife conservation.
Stevan Mirković (1927–2015) was a Yugoslav colonel general who served as Chief of the General Staff of the Yugoslav People's Army from 1987 to 1989.

Gwrgi and Peredur were 6th-century brothers and warriors in the Hen Ogledd (now Northern England and southern Scotland). They are best known for fighting in the Battle of Arfderydd in 573. Later, Geoffrey of Monmouth wrote of a Peredur in both his *Vita Merlini* and *Historia Regum Britanniae*. A Peredur is also the hero of the Welsh romance *Peredur son of Efrawg*.

Eucalyptus largiflorens, commonly known as black box, is a tree species native to Australia, reaching heights of up to 20 meters. It features dark grey, fibrous bark and lance-shaped leaves. The species is found primarily in grassy woodlands of the Murray–Darling basin.

The geoduck (*Panopea generosa*) is a large, burrowing saltwater clam native to the west coast of North America, primarily Washington, British Columbia, and Alaska. It is the world's largest burrowing clam, with some specimens weighing over 7 kilograms (15 lb) and living up to 140 years. The name "geoduck" comes from a Lushootseed word, either meaning "dig deep" or referencing its shape.  

Highly valued in Asian cuisine, particularly in China, Japan, and Korea, geoduck is often eaten raw, in soups, or as sashimi. Its commercial fishery, worth over $80 million, is tightly regulated in the U.S. and Canada. However, concerns over poaching, environmental impact, and contamination—such as a 2013 Chinese import ban due to toxins—have affected the industry.  

The geoduck's distinctive siphon has made it an internet curiosity and the mascot of Evergreen State College. Despite its popularity, wild populations recover slowly, with some areas requiring decades to replenish after harvesting.

HNoMS *Rauma* was a Norwegian Otra-class minesweeper launched in 1939 and commissioned in early 1940. Shortly after entering service, it participated in the defense of Horten during the German invasion of Norway, damaging enemy vessels before being seized by German forces. Renamed *Kamerun*, it served in the Kriegsmarine and later the German Mine Sweeping Administration. Returned to Norway post-war, *Rauma* was recommissioned in 1947, rebuilt as a minelayer training ship in 1949, and decommissioned in 1959 before being scrapped in 1963.

Design studies is an interdisciplinary field that examines both the internal practices of design and its external impacts on society, culture, and the environment. It encompasses various methodologies and theories, focusing on the meanings and consequences of design, including its ethical implications and role in shaping personal and cultural values. The field has evolved since the 1960s, integrating design history and research, and is represented by key journals such as *Design Studies* and *Design Issues*. Design studies also addresses contemporary issues like decolonization and the relationship between design and social factors, emphasizing the importance of understanding design as a complex, influential process.

"The 1900 House" is a historical reenactment reality television series that aired from December 28, 1999, to July 3, 2000, on Channel 4 in the UK and PBS in the US. The show features the Bowlers, a modern family who lived in a restored 1890s terraced house in Charlton, London, stripped of all modern conveniences, including electricity and indoor plumbing, to experience life as it was in 1900. The family was guided by historian Daru Rooke, who provided resources to help them adapt to the period lifestyle. The series' success inspired subsequent related productions and home video releases.

Connect Four is a two-player connection game first published by Milton Bradley (now owned by Hasbro) in 1974. Designed by Howard Wexler and Ned Strongin, the game involves players alternately dropping colored discs into a vertical grid, aiming to connect four of their discs in a row horizontally, vertically, or diagonally. If the grid fills without a winner, the game ends in a draw. 

The game has been mathematically solved, with the first player able to force a win under optimal play. Numerous variations exist, including different board sizes, gameplay rules, and themed editions. Connect Four has also been adapted into digital formats, arcade games, and large-scale outdoor versions, maintaining its popularity as a quick, strategic game for players of all ages.
Liu Junwei (born 1 May 1976) is a Chinese football manager and former defender, currently managing Suzhou Dongwu.
Judith Révész (1915–2018) was a Hungarian-Dutch ceramicist and sculptor known for her earthenware and bronze works.

Andrée Geulen (6 September 1921 – 31 May 2022) was a Belgian teacher and notable figure in the resistance during the Holocaust. Born in Schaerbeek, she became involved with the Committee for the Defence of Jews in 1943, where she helped protect Jewish children by placing them with Catholic families under false identities. Geulen's efforts included arranging for the care of approximately 300 Jewish children. After the war, she worked to reunite hidden children with their families and supported Jewish survivors. Recognized as Righteous Among the Nations in 1989, she received honorary Israeli citizenship in 2007 and was celebrated for her humanitarian contributions until her death at age 100.

The Habbari dynasty, also known as the Habbari Emirate, was an Arab dynasty that ruled over parts of Greater Sindh from 854 to 1011 CE, with its capital at Mansura. Established by 'Umar bin Abdul Aziz al-Habbari, the dynasty emerged during a period of weakened Abbasid authority, following the assassination of the pro-Abbasid governor of Sindh. The Habbari emirate controlled a region extending from the Arabian Sea to central Balochistan, while maintaining nominal allegiance to the Abbasid Caliphate. The dynasty facilitated trade routes through Mansura, enhancing its wealth and prominence. Over time, the Habbari rulers, initially Sunni Muslims, faced the rise of Ismaili missionary activity in the region. The dynasty ultimately fell to the Ghaznavids in 1026, marking the end of Habbari rule in Sindh.

Dingli (Maltese: Ħad-Dingli) is a village in the Northern Region of Malta, located on a plateau near the Dingli Cliffs, the highest point in Malta. Covering an area of 5.7 km², it had a population of 3,711 as of January 2019. The village is named after Sir Thomas Dingley, an English knight of the Order of St. John. Dingli has historical significance, with evidence of Phoenician, Carthaginian, and Roman settlements, including rock-cut tombs and Roman baths.

The parish, established in 1678, is dedicated to the Assumption of Our Lady, with its titular feast celebrated annually. Dingli saw significant development in the 20th century, including population growth and improved infrastructure.

Aadmi (1993), directed by Arshad Khan, follows Vijay Srivastav, an honest man falsely accused of murdering his family. Despite his innocence, he is jailed and later escapes to seek revenge against corrupt officials and terrorists. Shakti Kapoor plays a rare positive role as an honest police officer.

Sam Walker (born 16 June 2002) is an English-born Australian professional rugby league footballer who plays as a halfback or five-eighth for the Sydney Roosters in the NRL. The son of former player Ben Walker, he debuted in 2021 and won the Dally M Rookie of the Year award.

Vinita Marwaha Madill (born 9 January 1987) is a British space engineer and advocate for women in STEM. She has worked for the European Space Agency, German Aerospace Center, and TERMA B.V., specializing in astronaut training and space operations. In 2012, she founded *Rocket Women*, a platform promoting women in science. Recognized for her contributions, she has been featured in *Elle*, *Fast Company*, and *The Telegraph*.

Peter von Hess (29 July 1792 – 4 April 1871) was a German painter known for his battle scenes and landscapes. He trained in Munich and gained military experience during the Napoleonic Wars, later traveling to Greece to document the war of liberation.

Eddy Naït Slimani (born August 25, 1993) is a French professional kickboxer competing in the super bantamweight division. Active since 2013, he has achieved a record of 38 wins, including 8 by knockout, and 7 losses. Slimani has participated in various tournaments and held titles, including the La Nuit des Champions -65 kg title.

San José Castle (Spanish: *Castillo de San José*) was a military fort in Guatemala City. Construction began in 1843 and finished in 1846. It was built on the Buena Vista hill, which gave it a military advantage over the surrounding valley. In 1847, it became the most important ammunition warehouse in Guatemala. By 1852, it was also used as a prison. The fort was the site of heavy fighting during the revolt of April 1920, when forces loyal to Manuel Estrada Cabrera were defeated.

*Going Blank Again* is the second studio album by English shoegaze band Ride, released on 9 March 1992. Produced by Alan Moulder and the band, it features the singles "Leave Them All Behind" and "Twisterella." The album expanded on their debut's sound, blending shoegaze with psychedelic rock and power-pop influences. Critics praised its layered vocals and atmospheric guitars, and it has since been regarded as a key shoegaze release.

An avulsion injury occurs when a body structure is forcibly torn away, either partially or completely. Common types include skin avulsions (ranging from minor flaps to severe degloving), ear avulsions (often from bites or trauma), nail avulsions (where the nail detaches from the nail bed), and brachial plexus avulsions (nerve damage from trauma or birth complications). Treatment varies by severity, including suturing, grafts, or surgical reattachment. Surgical avulsions may also be performed to treat conditions like varicose veins or ingrown nails.

*Euprymna tasmanica*, commonly known as the southern dumpling squid or southern bobtail squid, is a species of bobtail squid in the family Sepiolidae. Native to Australian waters, it is characterized by its iridescent green or yellow coloration with brown spots, large semi-circular fins, and a butterfly-shaped light organ in its mantle cavity, which uses bioluminescent bacteria to counteract its silhouette. Its arms and tentacles are equipped with toothed suckers.

Reproduction involves females laying multiple batches of eggs, with hatchlings emerging highly developed but lacking the bacteria needed for their light organ, which they acquire from the water. Adults die shortly after reproduction. The species is listed as Data Deficient by the IUCN, and its common name is sometimes confused with a smaller, unnamed Australian bobtail squid species.

The Church for the Fellowship of All Peoples, established in 1944 by Howard Thurman in San Francisco, was the first racially integrated, intercultural church in the U.S. It continues to host community events, performances, and lectures, and annually honors contributors to community and social justice through the Howard Thurman Award.

Ernie Camacho (born February 1, 1956, in Salinas, California) was a right-handed pitcher who played in Major League Baseball from 1980 to 1990, primarily for the Oakland Athletics, Pittsburgh Pirates, Cleveland Indians, Houston Astros, San Francisco Giants, and St. Louis Cardinals. He recorded a win-loss record of 10–20, 45 saves, and an earned run average of 4.21. After his baseball career, Camacho worked in a school district in Salinas and supported causes related to Alzheimer's disease and youth baseball.

The Reformed Christian Church in Slovakia, historically part of the Reformed Church in Hungary, has roots in the Reformation of the 1520s. It currently serves approximately 110,000 members across 204 parishes and maintains educational institutions.

Barry Ono, born Frederick Valentine Harrison (21 May 1876 – ?), was a British comedian, songwriter, and performer known for his one-man routine "The Old Time Music Hall." He gained prominence in 1929 and published works like *The Great Barry Ono Song Book*. Later in life, he became an avid collector of penny fiction and operated a lending library as a child.

On April 21, 1789, George Washington arrived in Trenton, New Jersey, on his way to New York for his inauguration as the first President of the United States. He was greeted by a triumphal arch celebrating his Revolutionary War victories, adorned with laurel greenery, flowers, and banners. Local ladies lined the route, singing a special sonata and scattering flowers before him. This historic event was later re-enacted in 1989 and commemorated with a historic information sign near the Lower Trenton Bridge.

Our Revolution is a memoir by Bernie Sanders published in 2016, detailing his positions on climate change, free education, income inequality, and his role in the 2016 presidential election. Sanders shares his personal experiences from the campaign trail and outlines a progressive agenda aimed at transforming the country. Upon release, the book was a bestseller and received mixed reviews, with some praising its insights into Sanders' character and ideas.

EC KAC, founded in 1909 in Klagenfurt, Carinthia, Austria, is a professional ice hockey club that has dominated the Austrian Championship, winning 32 titles. The club competes in the ICE Hockey League and plays at the Stadthalle Klagenfurt arena. EC KAC has a rich history, including multiple European Cup appearances and a golden era of dominance from 1964 to 1980.

"The Fury of The Aquabats!" is the second studio album by the American ska band The Aquabats, released on October 28, 1997. The album features a blend of ska, surf rock, and punk, showcasing the band's comedic sensibility and eclectic musical experimentation. It includes notable tracks like "Super Rad!" and "My Skateboard!" and marks the first appearance of keyboardist Jimmy the Robot and the only album featuring drummer The Baron von Tito. The album received positive reviews and achieved minor commercial success, peaking at number 172 on the Billboard 200.
*Freerunner* is a 2011 action film about eight freerunners forced to participate in a deadly race for a million dollars.

The laminar flamelet model is a combustion model that treats a turbulent flame as a collection of thin, locally one-dimensional laminar flame structures. It simplifies complex turbulent combustion by assuming steady-state conditions, where species mass fractions and temperature depend on mixture fraction and scalar dissipation. The model is computationally efficient, capturing chemical reactions and molecular transport, but neglects transient effects and flame curvature. Variations like the transient laminar flamelet model (TLFM) address some limitations. Common applications include non-premixed combustion analysis, particularly in counterflow diffusion flames.

The Apostles' Fast is a fast observed by Eastern Orthodox, Coptic, Syriac, Maronite, and Melkite Christians. It begins after the Sunday of All Saints, which is eight weeks after Pascha (Easter), and continues until the Feast of Saints Peter and Paul on June 29. Its variable length, from 8 to 42 days, depends on the date of Pascha.

Tradition holds that the fast commemorates the apostles' fasting and prayer as they prepared to spread the Gospel. Fasting rules are less strict than during Great Lent or the Dormition Fast, typically involving abstention from meat, dairy, eggs, and sometimes fish, oil, and wine. The Feast of the Nativity of St. John the Baptist falls during this period.

The Jackson Plan, also known as the Raffles Plan, is a town plan for Singapore drawn up in 1822-1823. Following his return to Singapore in October 1822, Sir Stamford Raffles, the founder of modern Singapore, was displeased with the disorganised state of the settlement under William Farquhar's administration. Raffles formed a Town Committee to create a more structured urban plan, tasking Lieutenant Philip Jackson with drawing up the layout.

The plan divided Singapore into functional and ethnic subdivisions, with areas designated for European traders, Chinese, Indians, and Muslims. It also allocated land for administrative and commercial purposes. While the plan aimed for ethnic segregation, this was not strictly enforced, as evidenced by the presence of religious buildings from different communities within the same zones.

Raffles also made recommendations for building construction, including the use of masonry and roof tiles to reduce fire risk, and the creation of uniform facades with continuous sheltered walkways, which led to the distinctive five-foot ways seen in Singaporean and Malaysian architecture. Although not all aspects of the plan were immediately implemented, it laid the foundation for Singapore's urban development and the present-day Downtown Core.

Gareth James Delany (born 28 April 1997) is an Irish cricketer who plays as an all-rounder, batting right-handed and bowling right-arm leg break. He made his international debut for Ireland in 2019 in Twenty20 Internationals (T20Is) and in 2020 in One Day Internationals (ODIs). Domestically, he has played for Leinster Lightning, Leicestershire, and Munster Reds.

Connah's Quay Power Station, located in Flintshire, Wales, is an operational gas-fired combined cycle power station with a nameplate capacity of 1,420 MW. It was commissioned in 1996 and is owned by Uniper. The station replaced a previous coal-fired power station and is not to be confused with the nearby Deeside Power Station. It employs around 81 people and receives natural gas via an 18-mile pipeline from the Point of Ayr gas terminal.

Gene Winstead (born 1951) is an American politician who served as the mayor of Bloomington, Minnesota, from 2000 to 2020. A Republican, he was the city's longest-serving mayor, overseeing major projects including the expansion of the Mall of America, the Blue Line light rail, and infrastructure improvements. Before becoming mayor, he served on Bloomington's city council and planning commission.

Samuel Walker (born 16 June 2002 in Leeds, England) is an Australian rugby league player who plays as a halfback or five-eighth. He joined the Sydney Roosters in 2021, where he made his NRL debut and was named Dally M Rookie of the Year. Walker has since been a key player for the Roosters, contributing significantly to their performances in the 2022 season.

Henry I of Cyprus (5 May 1217 – 18 January 1253) was crowned king at age 8 in 1225, following a political maneuver by his uncle Philip to preempt a potential seizure of power by Frederick II, Holy Roman Emperor. He took control of the kingdom in 1232 upon reaching adulthood and maintained close ties with the Ibelin family. Henry served as Regent of Jerusalem from 1246 to 1253. He was married three times and had one son, Hugh II. Upon his death, Hugh II succeeded him as king. Henry was buried at the Church of the Templars in Nicosia.

William W. Arnold (August 28, 1843 – March 30, 1923) was an American physician and ornithologist known for his work in bird rehabilitation and conservation. Born in Indiana, he practiced medicine in Colorado Springs, where he and his wife operated a bird hospital, treating injured wild and domestic birds. An advocate for bird protection, Arnold wrote articles on ornithology, gave educational talks, and encouraged wildlife conservation. He was a member of the American Ornithologists' Union, the Cooper Ornithological Club, and the Colorado Audubon Society. His granddaughter, Berthe Arnold, also became a noted ornithologist. Arnold died in Colorado Springs and was buried in Evergreen Cemetery. His wife continued their bird hospital and established the Arnold Nature Club for children.

Rhizomatic learning is an educational concept inspired by the rhizome, a plant structure used by philosophers Gilles Deleuze and Félix Guattari as a metaphor for non-hierarchical, interconnected knowledge systems. It contrasts with traditional, linear models of learning, emphasizing organic, student-driven exploration. The concept has roots in John Dewey's ideas about conversational, interest-driven learning and has been further developed by feminist and critical pedagogy scholars. Rhizomatic learning has gained renewed attention in the context of online education, particularly in relation to MOOCs. However, it has faced criticism, with some questioning its practicality and its critique of formal education systems.

St Richard's Church is an active Church of England parish church located on Sydney Road in Haywards Heath, West Sussex. Founded in September 1937 by Reverend Thomas G. Wyatt, the church was consecrated in 1938 and is dedicated to Richard of Chichester. Designed by architect L. Keir Hett, the church features a unique blend of Art Deco and Scandinavian architectural styles and is constructed primarily of reinforced concrete. It was designated as a Grade II listed building on October 4, 2000. The parish was established in 1939, serving the northern part of Haywards Heath and surrounding areas.

*Crossing the Red Sea with The Adverts* is the 1978 debut studio album by English punk rock band The Adverts. Recorded at Abbey Road Studios, it received critical acclaim for its raw energy and is considered a landmark of the punk era. The album, featuring the single "No Time to Be 21," reached No. 38 on the UK Albums Chart and has been praised as one of the finest albums of the 1970s. It was later ranked No. 17 in *Mojo* magazine's list of the 50 greatest punk albums and included in *The Guardian*'s "1000 Albums to Hear Before You Die."

Bill Sizemore (born June 2, 1951) is an American political activist and former gubernatorial candidate in Oregon, known for his involvement in various tax-related ballot initiatives. A Republican, Sizemore founded Oregon Taxpayers United in 1993 and authored significant measures, including Measure 47, which rolled back property taxes and mandated a double majority for tax increases. He ran for Governor of Oregon in 1998 but lost to incumbent John Kitzhaber. Sizemore faced legal challenges, including a civil racketeering lawsuit resulting in fines for his organizations, and was later indicted for tax evasion in 2010. Despite these controversies, he continued to engage in political activities, including a 2010 gubernatorial campaign.

"Black Noise" is a 1994 book by Tricia Rose that critically examines rap music and its relationship with black culture, urban politics, and American society, receiving significant acclaim and an American Book Award.
The rusty-margined guan (Penelope superciliaris) is a species of bird in the family Cracidae. It is found in South America in a variety of landscapes. The IUCN has assessed it as near threatened.

Thomas Alexander Fraser, 12th Lord Lovat KT (17 June 1802 – 28 June 1875) was a Scottish peer and landowner. He served as Lord Lieutenant of Inverness from 1853 to 1873 and was made a Knight of the Thistle in 1865. Inheriting the Lovat estates in 1815, he was later confirmed as 12th Lord Lovat after the reversal of his ancestor's attainder.

The National Audit Office of Tanzania (NAOT) is an independent parliamentary body responsible for auditing the Government of Tanzania. It is headquartered in Dodoma and was formed in 2001, succeeding the Exchequer and Audit Department. The NAOT's mandate is derived from the Public Finance Act of 2001, revised in 2008. The current Auditor General of Tanzania is Charles Kichere.

The Petaluma and Santa Rosa Railroad (P&SR) was an interurban electric railroad that operated in Sonoma County, California, from 1904 to 1932. Formed through the consolidation of several horse car lines in 1903, it connected Petaluma, Santa Rosa, Sebastopol, and Forestville, with ferry service to San Francisco. The railroad faced significant challenges, including the "Battle of Sebastopol Road," a conflict with a competing steam railroad over track crossings. Despite early expansion and success, including freight and passenger services, the P&SR declined due to competition and economic shifts. It was acquired by the Northwestern Pacific Railroad in 1932, with operations ceasing by the mid-20th century.

Seneca Falls is a hamlet and census-designated place (CDP) in Seneca County, New York, located within the town of Seneca Falls. Originally incorporated as a village in 1831, it was dissolved on December 31, 2011, following a vote by residents. The area, historically inhabited by the Cayuga tribe, played a significant role in the Women's Rights Movement, hosting the 1848 Seneca Falls Convention, the first women's rights convention in the United States. The village was also a hub for early industrial development, utilizing water power from the Seneca River. Today, Seneca Falls is home to the Women's Rights National Historical Park and the National Women's Hall of Fame.

Gambling in New Jersey has a long history, with lotteries used to fund early colleges. While gambling was banned in 1897, racetrack gambling was re-legalized in 1939. Casino gambling was legalized in Atlantic City in 1976, and sports betting was legalized in 2018 after a Supreme Court ruling. Online gambling was legalized in 2013. The New Jersey Lottery and horse racing also exist.

Pelican Waters is a suburb in Caloundra, Queensland, established in 1995, with a population of 6,277 as of the 2016 census. It features predominantly canal estates and is located 7.7 kilometers southwest of Caloundra's CBD.

Borough Green & Wrotham railway station, located in Borough Green, Tonbridge and Malling, England, opened in 1874 as Wrotham and was renamed Borough Green & Wrotham in 1962. Managed by Southeastern, it has two platforms and experienced a decline in passengers from 0.432 million in 2017/18 to 78,272 in 2020/21. All services are operated by Southeastern using Class 375 and 377 EMUs.

Airblue is a Pakistani airline founded in 2003, commencing operations on June 18, 2004. Based in Islamabad, it operates from Jinnah International Airport and serves 26 destinations with a fleet of 12 aircraft. The airline is known for its innovative services, including e-ticketing and a frequent-flyer program called Blue Miles.

King Mongkut's Institute of Technology Ladkrabang (KMITL) is a public university established in 1960 in Bangkok, Thailand. Initially focused on telecommunications, KMITL has grown to include seven faculties and offers undergraduate and postgraduate programs. Supported by Japan through various cooperative agreements, KMITL has developed strong partnerships with both Japanese institutions and international entities, including Carnegie Mellon University. Known for its Research Center for Communication and Information Technology (ReCCIT) and Industry/University Cooperative Research Center in Data Storage Technology and Applications (iDSTA), KMITL plays a crucial role in Thailand's technological advancement.

Saint Vincent Martyr School, located in Madison, New Jersey, is a Roman Catholic elementary school serving students in pre-kindergarten through eighth grade. Established in 1848, the school has an enrollment of approximately 400 students. Saint Vincent Martyr School has received accreditation from the Blue Ribbon Schools Program and the Middle States Association of Colleges and Schools. The school offers a variety of sports programs, including basketball, baseball, softball, track and field, cross country, and cheerleading. A scout program is also available.

Filip Ingebrigtsen (born 20 April 1993) is a Norwegian middle-distance runner who specializes in the 1500 meters and mile. He won a bronze medal at the 2017 World Championships in the 1500m. At the 2016 European Athletics Championships, he won gold in the 1500 meters, with his older brother Henrik taking third place.

Ingebrigtsen has achieved personal bests of 3:30.01 in the 1500 meters and 3:49.60 in the mile, the former being a national record. He represented Norway at the 2016 Rio Olympics, but was disqualified in the 1500 meters trial heat. Some of his performances during the 2019 season were controversial, including a disqualification at the 2019 European Athletics Indoor Championships.

Entrenching battalions were temporary units formed during World War I by the British, Canadian, and New Zealand armies to perform labor-intensive tasks such as improving defenses and serving as reserve forces. The British Army established them in 1916, disbanding them in 1918 after the German spring offensive. The Canadian Expeditionary Force created similar units in 1916, disbanding them in 1917, while New Zealand formed entrenching battalions in 1918, disbanding them later that year.

Phebalium nottii, commonly known as the pink phebalium, is a shrub in the Rutaceae family, typically growing 1–3 meters tall. It features silvery to rusty-scaled branchlets, smooth leaves, and pink to mauve flowers arranged in umbels. This species is native to inland Queensland and parts of New South Wales.

Myrica cerifera, commonly known as wax myrtle, is a small tree or large shrub native to the southeastern and south-central United States, ranging into Central America. Adaptable to various habitats, it produces aromatic leaves and wax-coated berries that serve as food for numerous bird species. The plant is valued for its ornamental use, medicinal properties, and historical role in candle and soap making.

*I Even Met Happy Gypsies* is a 1967 Yugoslav film directed by Aleksandar Petrović. The story follows Beli Bora, a Romani man, and Tisa, a young woman, as they navigate love, conflict, and exile from their community. The film explores themes of Romani life and culture, with a cast led by Bekim Fehmiu, Olivera Vučo, and Gordana Jovanović. It was a critical success, winning the Special Grand Prize of the Jury and the FIPRESCI Prize at the 1967 Cannes Film Festival. It was also nominated for an Academy Award and a Golden Globe for Best Foreign Language Film.

Cape Newenham LRRS Airport (IATA: EHM, ICAO: PAEH, FAA LID: EHM) is a military airstrip owned by the U.S. Air Force. Constructed in 1951 to support the Cape Newenham Long Range Radar Station, it provides access for servicing and maintenance. The airstrip is not open to the public.

Yaser Abdel Said (born January 27, 1957) is an Egyptian-American former taxi driver convicted of the 2008 murders of his daughters, Amina and Sarah Said, in Irving, Texas. The murders, widely regarded as honor killings, were motivated by Said's anger over his daughters' perceived defiance of his strict control, including their relationships and independence. After the killings, Said evaded capture for 12 years, becoming one of the FBI's Ten Most Wanted Fugitives in 2014. He was apprehended in August 2020 in Justin, Texas, following years of investigation and assistance from his son, Islam Said, and brother, Yassein Said, who were later convicted of harboring a fugitive.

Said was convicted of capital murder in 2022 and sentenced to life imprisonment without parole. The case has drawn significant media attention and has been the subject of a 2014 documentary, *The Price of Honor*, and a 2015 episode of *The Hunt with John Walsh*. The murders and Said's subsequent flight have been cited as a high-profile example of honor-based violence in the United States. Said is currently incarcerated at the McConnell Unit in Texas, while his son and brother are serving federal prison sentences for their roles in aiding his evasion of justice.

*Minority Report* is an American science fiction crime drama television series that aired on Fox from September 21 to November 30, 2015. Developed by Max Borenstein, the series is a sequel to the 2002 film *Minority Report* and is based on Philip K. Dick's 1956 short story of the same name. Set in 2065, 11 years after the events of the film, the show follows Dash, a Precog with the ability to predict crimes, as he teams up with Detective Lara Vega to prevent future crimes while keeping his abilities hidden. Despite its connection to the acclaimed film, the series received mixed reviews and was canceled after one season, consisting of 10 episodes.

Braille pattern dots-1456 represents the voiceless dental fricative /θ/ in unified international braille. It is also used in some 8-dot braille systems like Gardner-Salinas and Luxembourgish Braille. In Japanese kantenji braille, related patterns are used with additional dots positioned above the base cell. This pattern is applied to all 6349 kanji in JIS C 6226-1978.
Gatillazo is a Spanish punk rock band formed in 2005 by Evaristo, former singer of La Polla Records, along with Txiki and Tripi.
Norway 1985, developed by Roger Keating and published by Strategic Simulations in 1985, is a wargame simulating NATO-Soviet forces in Norwegian arctic combat, available on Apple II and Commodore 64.

The Azerbaijan Carpet Museum (Azerbaijani: *Azərbaycan Xalça Muzeyi*) is a museum in Baku dedicated to Azerbaijani carpets and applied arts. Established in 1967, it houses over 10,000 items, including carpets, ceramics, jewelry, and textiles. The current building, designed to resemble a rolled carpet, opened in 2014. The museum also preserves carpets rescued from Shusha before its capture in 1992 and hosts international exhibitions. It plays a key role in promoting Azerbaijani cultural heritage.

The World Athletics Race Walking Team Championships, formerly known as the IAAF World Race Walking Cup, is a biennial racewalking competition organized by World Athletics. Inaugurated in 1961, the championships feature various race distances for both men and women. The event has been held in numerous locations around the world.

The 2016 edition, originally scheduled for Cheboksary, Russia, was relocated due to the IAAF's suspension of the All-Russia Athletic Federation. Similarly, the 2020 championships in Minsk, Belarus, were postponed due to the COVID-19 pandemic. Oman hosted the event in 2022.

The competition has faced issues with doping, leading to disqualifications of athletes. Olga Leonenko was the first to be disqualified in 1995, and Daniel Plaza became the first man in 1997. Several medalists have been stripped of their titles due to doping violations, including Vladimir Kanaykin, Sergey Kirdyapkin, and Alex Schwazer.

Clotilde von Derp (born Clotilde Margarete Anna Edle von der Planitz; November 5, 1892 – January 11, 1974) was a German expressionist dancer and a prominent figure in early modern dance. Known for her striking beauty and innovative style, she performed internationally with her husband, Alexander Sakharoff, blending avant-garde artistry with extravagant costumes.

Hazel Hawke AO (20 July 1929 – 23 May 2013) was the spouse of former Australian Prime Minister Bob Hawke from 1956 to 1995. She served as First Lady from 11 March 1983 to 20 December 1991. Known for her advocacy for reproductive rights and her role in promoting reconciliation and environmental causes, Hawke was appointed an Officer of the Order of Australia in 2001. In 2003, she publicly disclosed her battle with Alzheimer's disease. She passed away on 23 May 2013, leaving behind a legacy marked by her activism and personal struggles.
GE Power is a subsidiary of General Electric that manufactures power generation equipment, including gas turbines, nuclear reactors, and steam turbines.

*Saint Overboard* is a 1936 mystery novel by Leslie Charteris, part of *The Saint* series. The story follows Simon Templar, known as The Saint, as he rescues private detective Loretta Page during a pleasure cruise along the French coast. Loretta enlists Templar's help to stop a group of modern-day pirates, led by Kurt Vogel, who use advanced technology to loot sunken treasure. The novel features recurring characters from the series and was adapted into a 1995 radio play, though plans for a film adaptation were never realized.

Maria Silva Cruz, known as La Libertaria, was born on April 20, 1915, in Casas Viejas, Spain. A key figure in the Casas Viejas incident, she survived the 1933 conflict and was later executed by the Fascists in Ronda on August 23, 1936. Her son, Juan Perez Silva, worked to identify her remains, which were finally confirmed in 2010.

In particle physics, annihilation is the process that occurs when a subatomic particle collides with its respective antiparticle to produce other particles, such as photons, electrons, or other less massive particles. The total energy and momentum of the initial pair are conserved in the process and distributed among the resulting particles.

Annihilation often results in the production of photons. For example, when an electron annihilates with a positron (antielectron), the most probable outcome is the creation of two or more photons. The annihilation of heavier particles, such as protons and antiprotons, is more complex, resulting in the production of mesons, which subsequently decay into other particles.

Annihilation processes have various applications in physics, including positron emission tomography (PET) and high-energy physics experiments. In high-energy collisions of nucleons, the annihilation of sea quarks and gluons can lead to the production of Higgs bosons, as demonstrated by experiments at the Large Hadron Collider (LHC). Annihilation of antinucleons within atomic nuclei can also lead to disruption of the nucleus and the release of neutrons, potentially useful for spacecraft propulsion.
Mount Gould is a 13,011 ft peak in California's Sierra Nevada, first ascended in 1890.

Arthur Rackham (19 September 1867 – 6 September 1939) was a British illustrator best known for his distinctive fantasy artwork in children's literature. A leading figure of the early 20th-century "Golden Age" of book illustration, he gained fame for his intricate pen-and-ink drawings and watercolor paintings in works such as *Rip Van Winkle* (1905), *Peter Pan in Kensington Gardens* (1906), and fairy tales by the Brothers Grimm. His style, blending detailed linework with muted colors, influenced later artists and animation. Rackham's illustrations were widely exhibited, earning international acclaim, including gold medals at the Milan (1906) and Barcelona (1912) expositions. His legacy endures through reprints, greeting cards, and his impact on fantasy art.

SMS Eber was a 1,193-ton Iltis-class gunboat built for the German Imperial Navy in 1903. Commissioned later that year, she served in various African colonies and participated in the Agadir Crisis. During World War I, Eber was converted to an auxiliary cruiser but was scuttled in 1917 after Brazil entered the war on the Allied side.
Maria-Luise Rainer (born 23 April 1959) is an Italian former luger who won a bronze medal at the 1979 World Championships and the 1985–86 Luge World Cup.

AdGear is a digital advertising technology company known for its real-time advertising products, including a demand-side platform and ad servers. Founded in 2013, it powered ad delivery for LaPresse+ and became the first digital ad provider for the Government of Canada before being acquired by Samsung Electronics in 2016.

*Sanjog* is a 1972 Indian Hindi-language film, directed by S. S. Balan and produced by Ayaz Ali Shaikh. The film stars Mala Sinha, Amitabh Bachchan, and Aruna Irani. The film is about Mohan and Asha who get married but are separated by Mohan's mother.

"My Mowgli Boy" is a Chinese romance comedy about environmental protection, starring Ma Tianyu as Mo Ge Li, a boy raised by his grandfather in the forest, and Yang Zi as Ling Xi, an independent career woman. Mistakenly taken to the city, they live together and develop feelings for each other. Mo Ge Li learns to integrate into modern society, while Ling Xi learns environmental philosophy and joins ecological protection efforts. The series, praised for its environmental message and fashion sense, aired from August 29 to October 10, 2019, on iQiyi.
Yambio Airport is a public airport in South Sudan.

An antifuse is a type of electrical component that, when triggered, permanently changes from a non-conducting state to a conducting one. They are known for their use in miniature Christmas tree lights, where they short out a blown bulb to keep the string lit. Antifuses are also used to program integrated circuits (ICs), including programmable logic devices (PLDs) and programmable read-only memory (PROM).

The Czech units of measurement were traditional systems used in Bohemia, Moravia, Silesia, and Prague before the adoption of the metric system. These included units for length, such as the *stopa* and *loket*, and for area and capacity, like the *měřice* and *korec*.

Paris Gregory is a Byzantine illuminated manuscript of the *Homilies of Gregory of Nazianzus*, commissioned by Patriarch Photios I as a gift for Emperor Basil I. Celebrating the triumph of Orthodoxy over Iconoclasm, it features elaborate illustrations and symbolic imagery linking Gregory’s 4th-century sermons to contemporary theological debates. The manuscript, notable for its artistic sophistication, reflects Photios’s efforts to reinforce Orthodox tradition under Basil’s rule.

AAA vs MLW was a co-promoted professional wrestling event held on March 13, 2020, in Tijuana, Mexico, by Lucha Libre AAA Worldwide, Major League Wrestling, and Promociones EMW. The event, which aired from April 11 to May 9, 2020, featured nine matches between AAA and MLW wrestlers, continuing the working agreement between the two promotions initiated in August 2018. This was the second MLW event promoted in Mexico, following a previous show in 2019.

Two Paddocks is a boutique winery in Central Otago, New Zealand, founded in 1993 by actor Sam Neill. Specializing in Pinot Noir, the winery operates several vineyards, including the original First Paddock in Gibbston and additional sites in the Alexandra district. Known for its Proprietor's Reserve Pinot Noir and second label Picnic, Two Paddocks also produces Riesling, Sauvignon Blanc, and lavender oil.

Abha Dawesar (born January 1, 1974) is an Indian novelist and artist based in New York City, known for her award-winning novel "Babyji" (2005) and other works such as "Family Values" and "That Summer in Paris." A Harvard graduate, she also explores themes of digital technology and social behavior.

Raymond Cannon (born Ulises Tildmann Cannon; September 1, 1892 – June 7, 1977) was an American actor, film director, screenwriter, journalist, and author. He was active in film from 1913 to 1945, and later wrote books about sport fishing.
*CBS Playhouse* was an anthology drama television series that aired on CBS from 1967 to 1970. The show broadcast twelve teleplays over three seasons and won a Peabody Award in 1967.

Courtney Rashelle Wetzel, born on February 25, 1989, in Puyallup, Washington, is a former American soccer midfielder. She played college soccer for Oregon State University and professionally for teams including the Seattle Sounders Women, Ottawa Fury Women, SC Sand, and Portland Thorns FC. Wetzel also represented the United States women's national under-23 team.

Tobin Stokes (born 1966) is a Canadian composer from North Vancouver, British Columbia. He studied piano from age three and was a member of the Powell River Boy's Choir. Stokes holds degrees from the University of Victoria and the University of British Columbia. He has been composer-in-residence with the Victoria Symphony and at festivals in Powell River, British Columbia, and Örebro, Sweden. Stokes has composed the operas *Fallujah*, *Pauline*, *The Vinedressers*, and *Rattenbury*.
**Wilkinson Heights** is a census-designated place (CDP) in Orangeburg County, South Carolina, United States. The population was 1,946 at the 2020 census.

Teresa Ogilvy Dent, born in 1959, is a British CEO of the Game & Wildlife Conservation Trust, having served since 2001. She graduated from the University of Reading with a degree in agriculture and has held various leadership positions in conservation and agriculture. Dent was named a Commander of the Most Excellent Order of the British Empire (CBE) in 2015 for her services to wildlife conservation.

Gurie Georgiu (31 December 1968 – 21 October 2021) was a Romanian Orthodox bishop who served as the founding bishop of the Diocese of Deva and Hunedoara from November 2009 until his death. He was previously elected vicar of the Archdiocese of Craiova in 2001. Georgiu died from complications related to COVID-19 at the age of 52.

Seneca Falls is a hamlet and census-designated place (CDP) located in Seneca County, New York, within the town of Seneca Falls. Incorporated in 1831, it played a significant role in American history, particularly as the site of the first women's rights convention in 1848, organized by Elizabeth Cady Stanton and others. The hamlet is situated near the Seneca River and was historically known for its water-powered mills, becoming a major flour milling center in the 19th century. Seneca Falls was dissolved as a village on December 31, 2011, following a resident vote aimed at reducing property taxes. As of the 2020 census, the population was 6,809.

Gene Winstead (born 1951) is an American businessman and politician who served as the 12th mayor of Bloomington, Minnesota, from 2000 to 2020. A Republican, he was the city's longest-serving mayor, overseeing significant developments such as the expansion of the Mall of America, infrastructure improvements, and the introduction of organized garbage collection. Before his mayoral tenure, Winstead held various roles in local government, including positions on the Planning Commission and Housing and Redevelopment Authority. Known for his pragmatic approach, he prioritized economic growth, affordable housing, and transportation projects. Winstead retired from public office in 2020 after 35 years of public service.

"King Arthur's Disasters" is a 2005 British children's comedy series starring Rik Mayall, Matt Lucas, Morwenna Banks, and Phil Cornwell. Produced by Genevieve Dexter, the show aired on CITV from April 11, 2005, to February 1, 2006, spanning two seasons with 26 episodes. In each episode, King Arthur embarks on quests to obtain items requested by Princess Guinevere, only for something to go wrong, leaving Guinevere unsatisfied.
