The series features comedic depictions of classic Arthurian characters, including the rebellious knights Sir Lancelot and Sir Martyn, who share a common enmity towards Arthur.

DeForest, Wisconsin, a village in Dane County, is located at 43°14′45′′N 89°20′45′′W. With a population of 10,811 (as of 2020) and covering 8.57 square miles, DeForest is governed by a village president and municipal judge. The village was named for Isaac De Forest, who purchased the land in 1856. DeForest is known for its strong educational system, including the DeForest Area School District, and its proximity to transportation options like Interstate 39/90/94 and Dane County Regional Airport.

Ida S. Baker High School, a public secondary school in Cape Coral, Florida, was established in 2004 and named after Ida S. Baker, Lee County's first black female high school principal. Located at 3500 Agualinda Boulevard, the school serves grades 9–12 with an enrollment of 1,809 students (2018-19) and a student-to-teacher ratio of 21.25. Renowned for its diverse curriculum including Business, Engineering, Spanish, and ROTC, Ida S. Baker High School continues to honor Baker's legacy through educational awards.

Ahmad bin Ali Al Thani (1922 – November 25, 1977) was the Emir of Qatar from October 24, 1960, until his deposition on February 22, 1972. He ascended to the throne following his father's abdication and oversaw Qatar's independence from British rule in 1971. His reign was marked by economic growth due to oil discoveries and the establishment of modern administrative systems. Ahmad was deposed in a bloodless coup while abroad and lived in exile until his death in London, after which he was buried in Qatar.
Harrisburg, originally known as Harrisburgh, is a town in Lewis County, New York, United States. Established in 1803, it spans 39.9 square miles and had a population of 437 as of the 2010 census.
Ángel "Cucco" Peña (born September 1, 1948) is a Puerto Rican salsa composer, musician, singer, and record producer.

James Bouknight (born September 18, 2000) is an American professional basketball player for the Charlotte Hornets of the National Basketball Association (NBA). He played college basketball for the UConn Huskies.

Susan Sonnenschein is an American developmental psychologist and professor at the University of Maryland, Baltimore County (UMBC). She earned her Ph.D. from Stony Brook University and is a certified school psychologist. Since joining UMBC in 1979, she has taught courses in child development, cognitive development, and child assessment. Recognized for her mentorship, she has served on numerous dissertation and master's committees. In 2008, she received UMBC's Donald Creighton Award for Outstanding Faculty. Her research focuses on early childhood education and cognitive development.

*Ebirah, Horror of the Deep* (released in the United States as *Godzilla versus the Sea Monster*) is a 1966 Japanese kaiju film directed by Jun Fukuda and produced by Toho Co., Ltd. The film stars Akira Takarada, Kumi Mizuno, and Chotaro Togin, with special effects directed by Sadamasa Arikawa under the supervision of Eiji Tsuburaya. It is the seventh installment in the *Godzilla* franchise and follows a group of castaways who discover a terrorist organization, the Red Bamboo, enslaving natives on Letchi Island while using a giant lobster-like kaiju, Ebirah, to guard their operations. The group awakens Godzilla to battle Ebirah and ultimately thwart the Red Bamboo's plans.

Originally conceived as a King Kong film, the project was reworked to feature Godzilla after creative differences with Rankin/Bass. Released in Japan on December 17, 1966, the film was later distributed in the U.S. and gained cult status, including a feature on *Mystery Science Theater 3000*.

Hugh Leslie OBE (17 April 1900 – 2 September 1974) was an Australian politician and newspaper editor. A member of the Australian Country Party, he served as the federal Member of Parliament for Moore from 1949 to 1958 and again from 1961 to 1963. Born in Durban, South Africa, Leslie emigrated to Australia in 1923 and became active in politics after a career in journalism. He also served in the military during World War II, losing his right leg in Libya. Leslie was later recognized with an Order of the British Empire (OBE) in 1967.

Hugh Leslie OBE (17 April 1900 – 2 September 1974) was an Australian politician and newspaper editor. A member of the Country Party, he served in the Western Australian Legislative Assembly (1943–1949) and later represented Moore in the Australian House of Representatives (1949–1958, 1961–1963). He was awarded an OBE in 1967.

The Congo Pedicle is a salient of the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) that bisects the territory of Zambia. It originated from territorial negotiations between the British South Africa Company and King Leopold II's Congo Free State in the late 19th century, amid the Scramble for Africa. The absence of clear geographical features for a border led to the King of Italy drawing a line of longitude to define the boundary, resulting in the Pedicle.

The Anglo-Belgian Boundary Commission of 1911–1914 finalized the border. The Pedicle strategically isolates Zambia's Luapula Province and part of Northern Province from the Copperbelt. The Congo Crisis of the 1960s highlighted the Pedicle's impact, necessitating a long detour around Lake Bangweulu. It also exposes Zambia to conflict in the DRC and cross-border crime.

Nick Horvath (born February 18, 1981) is an American-New Zealand former professional basketball player. A 6'10" center/power forward, he played college basketball at Duke, winning an NCAA championship in 2001. He spent most of his professional career in Australia and New Zealand, winning an NBL title (2009) and NZNBL MVP (2012). He also briefly represented New Zealand internationally.

Luke Michael Haines (born 7 October 1967, Walton-on-Thames, Surrey, England) is an English singer-songwriter known for his work with bands like The Auteurs and Black Box Recorder. He has also pursued solo projects and collaborations, and has written books and a documentary about his career.

The architecture of Poland reflects the nation's historical and cultural evolution, spanning styles from Pre-Romanesque to contemporary designs. Key periods include Gothic (Wawel Cathedral, Malbork Castle), Renaissance (Wawel Castle, Zamość Old Town), Baroque (Wilanów Palace), and Neoclassical (Palace on the Isle). The 19th century saw eclecticism and Art Nouveau, while the interwar period embraced modernism and functionalism. Post-1945 architecture includes socialist realism (Palace of Culture and Science) and brutalism, followed by postmodern and neo-modern trends after 1989. Notable contemporary works include the Szczecin Philharmonic and Museum of the History of Polish Jews.

Face to Face, originally formed as Zero Tolerance in 1988, is an American alternative rock band known for their discography that includes 10 studio albums, 8 EPs, 3 live albums, 2 compilation albums, 12 singles, and 11 music videos. Formed in Riverside, California, the band has undergone several lineup changes and signed with various labels throughout their career. Notable albums include "Don't Turn Away," "Big Choice," and their self-titled fourth album. After a four-year hiatus, Face to Face reunited in 2008 and released "Laugh Now, Laugh Later" in 2011.

The Maasai Mara conservancies are privately managed wildlife conservation areas surrounding the Maasai Mara National Reserve in Kenya. These conservancies aim to balance wildlife conservation with sustainable tourism and the needs of the local Maasai communities. Tourism in the conservancies is highly regulated, with strict limits on tourist accommodations and safari vehicles, and activities such as night drives, off-road driving, and walking safaris are permitted, unlike in the national reserve. However, tensions have arisen between Maasai pastoralists and tourism operators due to issues like cattle grazing in restricted areas and allegations of unfair profit distribution and exclusion of local communities from decision-making processes, leading to growing distrust in conservancy management.

An ultrasonic motor operates by inducing dry friction in the stator to impart motion to the rotor through ultrasonic vibration. Two primary methods—traveling-wave and standing-wave vibration—are used to control friction and achieve bi-directional motion, with traveling-wave methods offering better efficiency and reduced wear. These motors can achieve peak vibration velocities of around 1 m/s, with displacements decreasing as frequency increases. They are used in various applications, including Canon's USM autofocus lenses, where they provide precise and long-lasting rotational movements. The technology continues to evolve, with ongoing research focusing on non-contact ultrasonic motors that use air or other fluids to transmit vibrations.

St Andrew's Church, Buckland Monachorum, is a Grade I listed Church of England parish church in Devon, England, dedicated to Saint Andrew. Originally medieval, it was extensively rebuilt in the late 15th century and restored in 1868–69. The church is notable for its Drake aisle monuments, including works by John Bacon Senior and Junior, and Richard Westmacott. It houses an organ installed in 1849, later expanded, and a peal of eight bells, five of which date to 1723.

The olfactory tract is a bundle of nerve fibers in the brain that transmits smell information from the olfactory bulb to the olfactory cortex. It divides into medial and lateral striae, ending in structures like the olfactory tubercle and amygdala. Damage to the tract can cause anosmia (loss of smell), as seen in Kallmann syndrome.

Melchior is the traditional name of one of the Magi (also known as the Three Kings or Three Wise Men) who visited the infant Jesus after his birth. He is often described as an old man and the King of Persia, who gave the gift of gold to signify Jesus' kingship. Melchior is venerated as a saint in several Christian denominations. His purported remains are buried in the Shrine of the Three Kings in Cologne Cathedral.

Osterheide is an unincorporated area in Heidekreis, Lower Saxony, Germany, with a population of 4,437 (as of 2022). Established in 1935 as part of the Bergen military training area, it became part of the NATO Bergen-Hohne firing ranges in 1958. Governed according to § 16 of the Lower Saxony Municipality Regulations, Osterheide's management is overseen by the Federal Government, with limited participation rights for residents.

Ranpak is an American packaging company specializing in recyclable paper-based cushioning and void-fill solutions. Founded in 1972 in Painesville, Ohio, it produces machines that convert kraft paper into protective packaging materials. Ranpak operates globally, with manufacturing in North America, Europe, and Asia. The company has undergone multiple ownership changes, including acquisitions by private equity firms, and went public in 2019. Recognized for innovation, it was named among *Fast Company*'s top logistics innovators in 2021 for its sustainable alternatives to plastic packaging. Ranpak is headquartered in Concord, Ohio.
Michael Spira is a British physician specializing in weight management, obesity, and general practice, currently serving as medical director of the Smart Clinics.

Alan Arnett McLeod (April 20, 1899 – November 6, 1918) was a Canadian pilot in the First World War, posthumously awarded the Victoria Cross for his bravery. Born in Stonewall, Manitoba, McLeod joined the Canadian and British Armies and served in the Royal Air Force. He died from the Spanish Influenza epidemic shortly after his 19th birthday. McLeod's sacrifice and heroism have been commemorated through various tributes, including streets, schools, and military units named in his honor.

The FIVB Senior Continental Rankings are a system established by the Fédération Internationale de Volleyball (FIVB) to evaluate national teams based on their performance in official competitions. In 2019, FIVB partnered with Hypercube Business Innovation to develop a new ranking platform that addresses issues with the previous calculation method, which favored countries participating in major events. The updated ranking system, implemented on February 1, 2020, incorporates match results from January 1, 2019, onward and is designed to be regularly updated, reflecting the latest performances of teams within their respective continental confederations.

The Colored Hockey League, founded in 1895 by black Baptist leaders and intellectuals in Halifax, Nova Scotia, Canada, aimed to attract young black men to church through hockey. With teams in seven Nova Scotia communities and one in Prince Edward Island, it featured innovations like goaltenders leaving their feet to cover pucks and the slapshot. The league left a lasting legacy, including a 2020 Canada Post postage stamp honoring its history.

*Seventeen* is a 1916 novel by Booth Tarkington. It tells the story of William Sylvanus Baxter, a middle-class teenager, who falls for Lola Pratt, an actress who is visiting his neighborhood. William's pursuit of Lola leads to humorous situations as he tries to appear more mature, much to the embarrassment of his family and friends. The novel was well-received upon publication, with critics praising its humorous and truthful portrayal of adolescence.

Erik Rauch (May 15, 1974 – July 13, 2005) was an American biophysicist, theoretical ecologist, and entrepreneur. He earned degrees from Yale University and MIT, where his research focused on evolving systems. Rauch co-founded MetaCarta and contributed to spatial information processing. He died in a hiking accident at age 31.
Jean Garrabé (1931–2020) was a French psychiatrist and historian of psychiatry.

The Reading Municipal Building is a historic civic building at 49 Pleasant Street in Reading, Massachusetts. Built in 1883, it was designed by Horace G. Wadlin in the Renaissance Revival and Stick/Eastlake styles. The building originally housed town offices, the fire station, and the local jail. It was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1984 and now serves as a senior center.

The Church of Nossa Senhora do Rosário is a Roman Catholic church in Topo, São Jorge, Azores, Portugal. Originally built in the 16th century, it was damaged by earthquakes in 1757 and 1980. Reconstructed in 1761, it features Baroque elements, including gilded retables and religious paintings.
"Fiction" is an EP by the band Suuns, released on October 30, 2020, featuring avant rock and experimental sounds, with singles including "Pray," "Breathe," and "Fiction."

Jean Messiha (born Hossam Boutros Messiha, September 10, 1970) is a French economist, civil servant, and far-right political commentator. Born in Cairo, Egypt, to a Coptic Christian family, he moved to France as a child and later became a naturalized citizen. Educated at Sciences Po and the École nationale d'administration, he worked as a high-ranking civil servant before joining Marine Le Pen's National Rally as an advisor. A vocal supporter of the Great Replacement conspiracy theory, he has criticized Islam and the European Union. After leaving the National Rally in 2020, he joined Éric Zemmour's Reconquête party in 2022. Messiha has been suspended from social media for violating hate speech policies.

Caserma Ederle is a United States Army base located in Vicenza, Italy, serving as a key installation for U.S. military operations in Europe. Established in 1955 as part of the U.S. Army Southern European Task Force (SETAF), the base has played a significant role in NATO operations and Cold War defense strategies, including the management of nuclear weapons stockpiles and regional tactical operations. Over the years, it has housed various infantry and support units, including elements of the 173rd Airborne Brigade Combat Team and the United States Army Africa headquarters.

The base provides extensive amenities for military personnel and their families, including housing, schools, and recreational facilities. Recent expansions include the construction of Caserma Del Din, a LEED-certified annex, to accommodate additional troops. Caserma Ederle remains a vital hub for U.S. military presence in southern Europe.

Andrea Debono (7 November 1821 – 29 October 1871) was a Maltese trader and explorer in Africa. Active in Sudan and Egypt, he became a British consular agent and explored the White Nile, including the Sobat River. Accused of slave trading, he later retired to Cairo, where he died.

The *Cello Concerto* by Esa-Pekka Salonen is a 2017 composition for solo cello and orchestra, incorporating live electronics. Written for cellist Yo-Yo Ma, the concerto is structured in three movements and explores themes of cosmic imagery and opposing forces, blending lyrical passages with dynamic, dance-like sections. Praised for its innovative orchestration and technical demands, the work has received mixed critical reception, with some highlighting its creativity and others questioning its emotional depth.
The Indian River is a river in Lanark County, Ontario, Canada. It begins at an unnamed lake in Lanark Highlands and flows into the Mississippi River in Mississippi Mills.

ETC Group, formerly RAFI (Rural Advancement Foundation International), is an eco-justice organization addressing social and environmental issues around new technologies. It opposes the adoption of genetic engineering in agriculture, patents on life, and biopiracy. The group campaigns against geoengineering and for increased regulation in synthetic biology, raising concerns about corporate involvement and biosafety.

Clarke v Earl of Dunraven [1897] AC 59, also known as The Satanita, was a UK House of Lords case regarding contract law and sailing. It concerned a collision between yachts entered in a race, where one yacht, *The Satanita*, sank *Valkyrie II*. The House of Lords upheld the Court of Appeal's decision that the owner of *The Satanita* was liable to pay compensation.

Paul Speratus (1484–1551) was a Lutheran minister, hymn writer, and disciple of Martin Luther. He helped create the first Lutheran hymnal, *Achtliederbuch*, and wrote the hymn "Es ist das Heil uns kommen her" while imprisoned.

Luke Haines (born 7 October 1967) is an English singer-songwriter best known as the frontman of the alternative rock band The Auteurs and the electronic group Black Box Recorder. His solo work spans rock, electronica, and experimental music.

Nakae Chōmin (1847–1901) was a Japanese journalist, politician, and philosopher. He was born in Kōchi Prefecture. Nakae is known for his role in developing liberalism in Japan. He translated works by Jean-Jacques Rousseau into Japanese and propagated Western democratic ideas through his newspapers, including the "Oriental Free Press." Nakae also served in the Diet of Japan.

Lefty Leifield (September 5, 1883 – October 10, 1970) was a left-handed pitcher who played in Major League Baseball from 1905 to 1920. He won 124 games and achieved a 2.47 ERA, including a World Series championship with the 1909 Pittsburgh Pirates.
Buck Brothers were a pop punk/alternative rock band from London and Jersey, active from 2005 to 2011. They later changed their name to Top Buzzer.
"Like a Man" is a 2017 tropical house song by Onhel featuring Lil Wayne. Produced by Onhel, it was officially released after being leaked online.

2002 Euler, designated (2002) Euler, is a minor planet named after the Swiss mathematician Leonhard Euler. Discovered on August 29, 1973, by T. Smirnova at the Crimean Astrophysical Observatory, it orbits the Sun with a semi-major axis of 2.4178 AU and an orbital period of 3.76 years. Classified as a main-belt asteroid, Euler has a diameter of approximately 19.78 km and an albedo of 0.0375.

Dick Anderson (born July 29, 1941) is an American former football player and coach. He played tight end and defensive end at Penn State (1960–1962) before becoming a longtime assistant under Joe Paterno (1973–1983, 1990–2011). He also served as head coach at Rutgers (1984–1989), compiling a 28–33–4 record. Anderson coached multiple All-American offensive linemen at Penn State, including Pro Football Hall of Famer Mike Munchak.

The pluperfect is a tense that combines past tense and perfect aspect, typically used to describe an action that was completed before another past action. In modern linguistic terms, it is classified as an absolute-relative tense. It is formed differently across various languages, often using auxiliary verbs or inflections. For instance, in English, it is formed with "had + past participle," while in Latin, it is formed without an auxiliary verb in the active voice and with an auxiliary verb plus the perfect passive participle in the passive voice. The pluperfect is used to clarify the temporal relationship between events in the past, ensuring that the sequence of events is accurately conveyed.

The 1988 San Francisco Giants season was the franchise's 106th season and their 31st in San Francisco. The team played their home games at Candlestick Park and competed in the National League's Western Division. Managed by Roger Craig and owned by Bob Lurie, the Giants' games were broadcast on KTVU and KNBR, with additional coverage on GiantsVision and KIQI.

Petro Pysarchuk (born June 6, 1955) is a Ukrainian politician, entrepreneur, and former member of the Verkhovna Rada, representing the Party of Regions. He also founded Lviv's largest trade complex, Pivdennyj Market.

The Leader of Alderney is the highest civil figure in Alderney, elected for a four-year term. Historically, Alderney has had various leaders, starting with William L'Ingenieur in 1042, who was ennobled as Lord of Alderney. The role evolved through different titles and administrations, including the Governorship, which ended in 1862. After World War II, Alderney became part of the Bailiwick of Guernsey, and a democratically elected President of the States of Alderney serves as the Leader of Alderney.

Nodes of Ranvier is an American metalcore and Christian metal band formed in September 2000 in Sioux Falls, South Dakota. Active until 2008 and then reuniting in 2017, the band has undergone numerous lineup changes, with 18 different members over its history. They released three albums through Facedown Records before signing with Victory Records in 2007. Despite initial excitement, their relationship with Victory soured, leading to their disbandment in December 2007. The band reunited for Facedown Fest 2017.

Buck Gurley (born April 7, 1978) is a former American football defensive tackle and current coach. He played college football at the University of Florida and went undrafted in 2001, later signing with the Miami Dolphins, Chicago Bears, and Tampa Bay Buccaneers, where he was part of the Super Bowl XXXVII championship team.

Port Dock railway station was a historic railway station in Port Adelaide, South Australia, located at the intersection of St Vincent Street and Lipson Street. Opened in 1856 as Port Adelaide station, it served as the town's primary railway station until 1916, when it was renamed Port Dock following the opening of the Port Adelaide Commercial Road station. The station played a significant role in connecting goods and passengers to the port, with various lines extending to wharves and private sidings. Declining use led to its closure in 1981, and the site was later redeveloped to house the National Railway Museum. Plans for a new station near the original site were proposed but remain on hold.

Ribadesella is a municipality in the autonomous community of Asturias, Spain. Located on the coast, it is known for the Tito Bustillo Cave, which contains prehistoric wall paintings dating back approximately 29,000 years and is part of the Paleolithic Cave Art of Northern Spain UNESCO World Heritage Site. The first written references to the settlement date to the first century BC.

The town of Ribadesella is characterized by its two main streets, the Gran Vía de Agustin Argüelles and the Calle Comercio, as well as the Casco Antiguo (old quarter) and the Paseo Marítimo del Puerto (Maritime Avenue of the Port). A major cultural event is the annual International Descent of the Sella, a kayak race accompanied by the Fiesta de Les Piragües. Numerous other festivals celebrate local traditions, food, and religious figures.

Substance Church, founded in 2004 by Pastor Peter Haas, is a youthful megachurch known for its contemporary worship style and innovative use of media. Initially holding services at the University of Minnesota, it later established permanent locations in Spring Lake Park and downtown Minneapolis. The church emphasizes classic Evangelical beliefs with Neocharismatic elements and is recognized for its church planting efforts and vibrant music ministry.

The Robinsons Summit Center is a 38-story office skyscraper located on Ayala Avenue in Makati, Philippines. Completed in 2001, the 174-meter (570.87 ft) building was designed by Hellmuth, Obata + Kassabaum in collaboration with W.V. Coscolluela & Associates. Owned by Robinsons Land Corporation, it features a double-height lobby, advanced elevator systems, a helipad, and amenities like a Fitness First branch and ample parking.

Nactus kunan, classified as Nactus kunan Zug and Fisher, 2012, is a gecko species found on Manus Island, classified under the family Gekkonidae. Known from two specimens, it is brightly colored with distinct banding and a unique head pattern. Classified as Data Deficient by IUCN, it is expected to occur more widely in the island's highland forests.
KeeWeb is a free, open-source password manager developed by Dimitri Witkowski. Written in JavaScript, it is cross-platform and supports synchronization with popular cloud storage services.

The Berlin Green Head is an ancient Egyptian greenschist statue from the Ptolemaic Period (100–50 BCE), now housed in the Egyptian Museum of Berlin. Measuring 21 cm in height, it depicts a realistic, symmetrical face of a middle-aged man with detailed wrinkles. Originally believed to reflect Greek anatomical knowledge, its style was later linked to earlier Egyptian works. The uninscribed statue's provenance remains unknown, and it was acquired in 1895 from private collections. It is often compared to the similar Boston Green Head.

The 354th Fighter Squadron, nicknamed the Bulldogs, has served the United States Air Force from 1942 to 1946, 1952 to 1958, 1962 to 1982, and since 1991. It has flown combat missions in the European Theater of Operations and Vietnam and conducted training in forward air control and combat crew training. In February 2015, the squadron deployed A-10 Thunderbolts to Spangdahlem Air Base in support of Operation Atlantic Resolve.

Charles Jackson "Bud" Wildman (born June 3, 1946) is a Canadian politician. He was an Ontario New Democratic Party (NDP) member of the Legislative Assembly of Ontario for Algoma from 1975 to 1999. He served as a cabinet minister in the government of Bob Rae and as interim party leader in 1996.

The 1944 explosion in Aarhus occurred on July 4, 1944, at the Port of Aarhus in Denmark, when a barge loaded with 150 tonnes of ammunition detonated at 13:47. The explosion generated a massive column of water and was heard up to 20 kilometers away, causing significant destruction in the surrounding area. It resulted in the deaths of 39 individuals, including 33 Danes, primarily dock workers, and injured approximately 250 others. The blast also caused extensive damage to buildings and infrastructure, with debris raining down over a kilometer radius. Following the incident, German authorities attributed the explosion to communist sabotage, a claim contested by Danish labor unions. A memorial service was held at Aarhus Cathedral, and a monument was erected in Vestre Cemetery in December 1945 to honor the victims, 12 of whom were buried in a mass grave. Donations to support the victims' families totaled 522,000 kroner.

John Pierrakos (February 8, 1921 – February 1, 2001) was a Greek-American psychiatrist known for founding Bioenergetic Analysis, a therapeutic approach integrating physical and emotional healing. After emigrating to the United States during World War II, he earned his M.D. from the University of the State of New York in 1947 and worked in various psychiatric roles, including in the U.S. Navy. Pierrakos co-founded The Bioenergetics Institute with Alexander Lowen in 1955, later establishing The Center for the New Man, which evolved into The Pathwork Center, emphasizing holistic healing through the integration of body and consciousness.

Cuneo railway station, located in Piazzale della Libertà, Cuneo, Italy, opened on November 7, 1937. Operated by Rete Ferroviaria Italiana Centostazioni, it serves the Fossano–Cuneo, Savigliano–Cuneo, Cuneo–Ventimiglia, and Cuneo–Mondovì lines. The station handles about 3 million passenger movements annually and is equipped with modern information systems. It is also connected to urban and suburban bus services.

The Doughnut is the nickname for the headquarters of the UK Government Communications Headquarters (GCHQ), a British intelligence and security organization responsible for providing signals intelligence and information assurance to the government and armed forces. Located in Benhall, Cheltenham, Gloucestershire, England, the building consolidated GCHQ's operations from over 50 buildings into a single location when it was completed in 2003.

Designed by architect Chris Johnson of Gensler and constructed by Carillion, the Doughnut is noted for its circular shape and structural expressionist style. The building is divided into three identical four-story structures connected at the top and bottom, with a central open-air courtyard. The design was intended to foster collaboration among staff.

The construction of the Doughnut was funded by a private finance initiative (PFI) and cost £337 million to build, though the overall PFI contract was £1.2 billion. The project faced criticism due to significant cost overruns, primarily attributed to the complexity of transferring GCHQ's computer network. The building was officially opened by Queen Elizabeth II in 2004. The Doughnut has been designated as a protected site under the Serious Organised Crime and Police Act 2005. GCHQ staff also participate in charitable efforts in the local community.

Franco-Albertans are French Canadians living in the province of Alberta. They are the fourth-largest French-speaking population in Canada, after Quebec, Ontario, and New Brunswick, and the largest in Western Canada. In 2016, 86,705 Albertans reported French as their mother tongue, with 264,715 reporting being bilingual in English and French. The Franco-Albertan community is largely composed of migrants, with only 25% born in the province. The majority reside in Greater Edmonton and the Calgary Region.

Historically, French Canadians worked as fur traders in Alberta in the late 18th century. French language education was introduced in the early 19th century by French missionaries. Although French was initially an official language in the North-West Territories, it was later removed. In 1925, the Association canadienne-française de l’Alberta was formed to advocate for linguistic rights. The enactment of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms in 1982 led to the establishment of francophone schools, and in 1994, five regional francophone school boards were created. In 1988, English was declared Alberta's official language, but French is permitted in the legislature and judicial system. In 2017, Alberta established its first French language policy and adopted the Franco-Albertan Flag.

Franco-Albertan culture is maintained through various organizations, including L’Unithéâtre and the Centre d’arts visuels de l’Alberta, and celebrated at the annual Franco-Alberta Festival. Media services are provided by Société Radio-Canada and community radio stations, as well as the weekly newspaper Le Franco.

Pa amb tomàquet (Catalan pronunciation: [ˈpa əm tuˈmakət], meaning "bread with tomato") is a traditional food in Catalan, Balearic, and Aragonese cuisines in Spain. It dates back to the 18th century, with the first written reference appearing in 1884. It is made by rubbing half a ripe tomato over toasted bread and flavoring it with olive oil and salt. Optionally, garlic can be rubbed on the bread before the tomato.

Pa amb tomàquet is often served as a tapa or snack, accompanying sausages, ham, cheeses, omelets, anchovies, or grilled vegetables. In Majorca, it is prepared with a specific variety of tomato called Tomàtiga de Ramellet.

KGGF (690 AM) is a radio station broadcasting a talk radio format. Licensed to Coffeyville, Kansas, it serves Southeast Kansas, along with parts of Oklahoma, Missouri, and Arkansas. The station is owned by Sek Media, LLC, and is a sister station to KGGF-FM. KGGF's studios are located in Coffeyville.

KGGF broadcasts local news, farm reports, and talk shows on weekdays, with nationally syndicated conservative hosts in afternoons and nights. Weekend programming includes shows on gardening, technology, and old-time radio. KGGF first went on the air in 1930. In 1941, KGGF was reassigned to 690 kHz.

"Songs of the Pixies" is a poem by Samuel Taylor Coleridge, written in 1793 during his summer vacation in Devon. Inspired by local folklore, it describes pixies in a woodland setting and reflects Coleridge's early poetic style. The poem was included in his 1796 collection *Poems on Various Subjects*.

Queen's Fort Military Museum, located in Bloemfontein, South Africa, was originally built in 1848 to honor Queen Victoria and later served various military and administrative roles. It has been a military hospital, prison, and headquarters for different organizations, including the South African Constabulary and the Union Defence Force. Today, it functions as a military museum showcasing the region's military history from 1820 to the struggle against apartheid.

The 2017 Ole Miss Rebels football team represented the University of Mississippi in the 2017 NCAA Division I FBS football season. Under the leadership of first-year head coach Matt Luke, the team finished with a record of 6–6, including a 3–5 mark in Southeastern Conference (SEC) play. The season was marked by significant coaching staff changes, including the hiring of Wesley McGriff as defensive coordinator. Additionally, the program faced challenges due to an ongoing NCAA investigation, which resulted in 21 charges, including 15 Level I violations, leading to self-imposed penalties such as a postseason ban and scholarship reductions. The Rebels played their home games at Vaught–Hemingway Stadium and faced a schedule that included both SEC and non-conference opponents.

The 1982 Massachusetts elections were held on November 2, electing state executives including Governor Michael Dukakis and Senator Ted Kennedy, as well as deciding four ballot measures on education funding, the death penalty, nuclear power, and bottle deposits.

The Sanctuary is a late Neolithic and early Bronze Age stone and timber circle located on Overton Hill near Avebury in Wiltshire, England. Part of the Stonehenge, Avebury and Associated Sites UNESCO World Heritage Site, it was constructed in several phases between 3000 and 2000 BCE, evolving from a small timber structure into concentric rings of posts and stones. The site connects to Avebury via the West Kennet Avenue, a 2.4-kilometer stone avenue, suggesting a ceremonial or processional link between the two monuments.  

Excavations in the 1930s revealed postholes and stone settings, indicating multiple construction phases, though much of the site was destroyed in the 18th century. Antiquarian William Stukeley recorded its appearance before its demolition, depicting it as part of a larger ritual landscape. The Sanctuary's exact purpose remains uncertain, with theories ranging from a mortuary house to a ceremonial gathering place. Today, its layout is marked by concrete posts, though little visible evidence of the original structure remains.

The Wenceslas Bible, commissioned by Wenceslaus, King of the Romans and Bohemia, and illuminated by Frána Kuthner in the 1390s, is an incomplete German translation of the Bible. Originating in Prague, it features 654 miniatures on 1,214 parchment leaves, measuring 530 x 365 mm. Although Wenceslas' father banned vernacular Bible translations, the king's patronage of this lavish edition highlights the close relationship between the Bohemian royal court and the emerging Reformation. The Bible, though not fully completed, surpasses other medieval manuscripts in length, dimensions, and artistic detail.

The first USS Arizona was an iron-hulled, side-wheel merchant steamship. Seized by the Confederate States of America in 1862 during the American Civil War, she was captured later the same year by the United States Navy.

The Oregon State University Foundation is an American private nonprofit corporation dedicated to enhancing the mission of Oregon State University in Corvallis, Oregon. The OSU Foundation is governed by a volunteer Board of Trustees.
Microsoft DNS is the name given to the implementation of domain name system services provided in Microsoft Windows operating systems.

Stowaway is a 1936 American musical drama film directed by William A. Seiter. The screenplay by William M. Conselman, Nat Perrin, and Arthur Sheekman is based on a story by Samuel G. Engel. The film is about a young orphan called "Ching Ching" (Shirley Temple) who meets wealthy playboy Tommy Randall (Robert Young) in Shanghai and then accidentally stows away on the ocean liner he is travelling on. The film was hugely successful, and is available on videocassette and DVD.

Patricia "Patt" Murphy Derian (née Murphy; August 12, 1929 – May 20, 2016) was an American civil rights and human rights activist who fought racism in Mississippi and went on to serve as Assistant Secretary of State for Human Rights and Humanitarian Affairs from 1977 to 1981. She was, remembered The Times of London, "a courageous champion of civil rights who took on some of the world's most brutal dictators in her role as a senior American diplomat."

General Motors (Thailand) Limited (GMT) was a holding company of sales and manufacturing subsidiaries of General Motors (GM) in Thailand. The company was registered in 1993 as a sales company and opened its manufacturing plant in 2000. At its height, GM Thailand exported vehicles to most regions in the world, including South America, Central America, Europe, Africa, the Middle East, Southeast Asia, Australia and Japan. In February 2020, GM announced that it would withdraw from the Thai market and the Rayong plant would be acquired by Great Wall Motors by the end of 2020. The company continued to support existing Chevrolet owners for ongoing aftersales, warranty and service.

I Even Met Happy Gypsies is a 1967 Yugoslav film by Serbian director Aleksandar Petrović. Its original Serbian title is Skupljači perja, which means The Feather Gatherers. The film is centered on Romani people's life in a village in northern Vojvodina, but it also deals with subtler themes such as love, ethnic and social relationships. Beside Bekim Fehmiu, Olivera Vučo, Bata Živojinović and Mija Aleksić, film features a cast of Romani actors speaking the Romani language. I Even Met Happy Gypsies is considered one of the best films of the Black Wave in Yugoslav cinema.

Rupa Goswami (Lang-sa, Lang-bn, IAST: Rūpa Gosvāmī; 1489–1564) was a devotional teacher (guru), poet, and philosopher of the Gaudiya Vaishnava tradition. With his brother Sanatana Goswami, he is considered the most senior of the six Goswamis of Vrindavan associated with Caitanya Mahaprabhu, a hidden avatar (incarnation) of Krishna in Kali Yuga.

Mesa/Boogie (also known as Mesa Engineering) is an American company in Petaluma, California, that manufactures amplifiers and other accessories for guitars and basses. It has been in operation since 1969.

Mesa was started by Randall Smith as a small repair shop which modified Fender amplifiers, particularly the diminutive Fender Princeton. Smith's modifications gave the small amps much more input gain, making them much louder as well as creating a high-gain, distorted guitar tone. Prominent early customers included Carlos Santana, and Ron Wood and Keith Richards of The Rolling Stones. Exposure from these top players helped to establish Mesa/Boogie's position on the market, and it is frequently referred to as the first manufacturer of boutique amplifiers.

Jasmina Obradović (Serbian Cyrillic: Јасмина Обрадовић; born 29 January 1961) is a politician in Serbia. She has served in the National Assembly of Serbia since 2012 as a member of the Serbian Progressive Party.

Woodall services is a motorway service station on the M1 motorway in Rotherham close to Sheffield in England. It lies between junctions 30 and 31. It was opened in 1968 by Trust House Forte but was renamed Welcome Break after the takeover of the company. It takes its name from the nearby village of Woodall.

Seneca Falls is a hamlet and census-designated place in Seneca County, New York, United States. The population was 6,681 at the 2010 census. The 2020 census population of Seneca Falls CDP was 6,809. The hamlet is in the Town of Seneca Falls, east of Geneva. It was an incorporated village from 1831 to 2011.

Finger Lakes Regional Airport (0G7) is south of the hamlet.

Seneca Falls was the site of the Seneca Falls Convention, a foundational event in the Women's Rights Movement. The town is believed by some to have been the inspiration for the fictional town of "Bedford Falls, N.Y." portrayed in filmmaker Frank Capra's classic 1940s film "It's a Wonderful Life".
Air Vice Marshal Christina Reid Elliot, CB, CBE, DL is a retired senior Royal Air Force officer.

Norma Aleandro (born 2 May 1936) is an Argentine actress. She is considered one of the most celebrated and prolific Argentine actresses of all time and is recognized as a cultural icon in her home country.

Aleandro starred in the 1985 film The Official Story, a role that earned her the Cannes Award for best actress. She has performed in other successful films like The Truce (1974), Cousins (1989), Autumn Sun (1996), The Lighthouse (1998), Son of the Bride (2001) and Cama Adentro (2005). For her performance as Florencia Sánchez Morales in the 1987 film Gaby: A True Story, she received a Golden Globe nomination and an Academy Award nomination for Best Supporting Actress.

Aleandro wrote the 1970 film The Inheritors and performed in various plays such as August: Osage County. Aleandro also appeared in the Argentine adaptation of BeTipul, the critically successful En terapia.

Euprymna tasmanica, also known as the southern dumpling squid or southern bobtail squid, is a bobtail squid that lives in the shallow (0.5 m to at least 80 m) temperate coastal waters of southern Australia's continental shelf. It lives for between 5 and 8 months and the adults can grow up to 6 or 7 cm long with a mantle length of 3 to 4 cm. They are found in seagrass beds or areas with soft silty or muddy bottoms from Brisbane on the east coast to Shark Bay on the west, as well as around Tasmania. Southern dumpling squid are nocturnal and during the day hide in sand or mud covered in a mucus-lined coat of sediment. If disturbed acid glans can quickly remove this coat as an additional decoy to ink squirting.

Maitreya-nātha (c. 270–350 CE) is a name whose use was pioneered by Buddhist scholars Erich Frauwallner, Giuseppe Tucci, and Hakuju Ui to distinguish one of the three founders of the Yogacara school of Buddhist philosophy, along with Asanga and Vasubandhu. Some scholars believe this Maitreya to be a historical person in India. The traditions themselves have held that it is referring to Maitreya, the future buddha.

The Lady in the Lake is a 1943 detective novel by Raymond Chandler featuring the Los Angeles private investigator Philip Marlowe. Notable for its removal of Marlowe from his usual Los Angeles environs for much of the book, the novel's complicated plot initially deals with the case of a missing woman in a small mountain town some 80 miles (130 km) from the city. The book was written shortly after the attack on Pearl Harbor and makes several references to America's recent involvement in World War II.

George Richmond RA (28 March 1809 – 19 March 1896) was an English painter and portraitist. In his youth he was a member of The Ancients, a group of followers of William Blake. Later in life he established a career as a portrait painter, which included painting the portraits of the British gentry, nobility and royalty.

He was the son of Thomas Richmond, miniature-painter, and was the father of the painter William Blake Richmond as well as the grandfather of the naval historian, Admiral Sir Herbert Richmond.

A keen follower of cricket, Richmond was noted in one obituary as having been "an habitué of Lord's since 1816".
E11EVEN Miami is a multilevel nightclub located in downtown Miami. The night club was opened February 5, 2014.
Odostomia farallonensis is a species of sea snail, a marine gastropod mollusc in the family Pyramidellidae, the pyrams and their allies.

Bubble sort, sometimes referred to as sinking sort, is a simple sorting algorithm that repeatedly steps through the input list element by element, comparing the current element with the one after it, swapping their values if needed. These passes through the list are repeated until no swaps had to be performed during a pass, meaning that the list has become fully sorted. The algorithm, which is a comparison sort, is named for the way the larger elements "bubble" up to the top of the list.

This simple algorithm performs poorly in real world use and is used primarily as an educational tool. More efficient algorithms such as quicksort, timsort, or merge sort are used by the sorting libraries built into popular programming languages such as Python and Java.

GLtron is a 3D snake game based on the light cycle portion of the film Tron. The game is free and open-source software and has been ported to many mobile and non-mobile operating systems such as Windows, MacOS, Symbian and Android over the years.
Crossing the Red Sea with The Adverts is the debut studio album by English punk rock band the Adverts. It was released on 17 February 1978 by record label Bright.

Grace Yun Yi is a professor of the University of Western Ontario where she currently holds a Tier I Canada Research Chair in Data Science. She was a professor at the University of Waterloo, Canada, where she holds a University Research Chair in Statistical and Actuarial Science. Her research concerns event history analysis with missing data and its applications in medicine, engineering, and social science.

James McLachlan Turnbull (born 23 May 1882) was a Scottish footballer who played as a forward, most notably for Manchester United, with whom he won the Football League and the FA Charity Shield in 1908 and the FA Cup in 1909. He was the first player to net a hat-trick in a Charity Shield game.

Dejarte de Amar (English: Stop Loving You) is the second studio album recorded by Mexican pop rock band Camila, It was released by Sony Music Latin on February 9, 2010 (see 2010 in music) Produced by band leader Mario Domm and mostly written by Domm and Mónica Velez, the album became a success in the United States and Mexico. The album was certified Disco Platino in the United States by the RIAA and 4× Platinum in Mexico by AMPROFON. It was recorded in Mexico at Mamita Studio with theme about love, loneliness and lies. The album spawned three singles: "Mientes", "Aléjate de Mi", and "Bésame", all of which reached number-one on the Billboard Latin Pop Airplay chart. The album received generally positive reviews for its arrangements and compositions.

The album reached number-one on the Billboard Top Latin Albums and Latin Pop Albums in the United States and number-one on the Mexican Albums Chart. It received a Latin Grammy award for Best Pop Vocal Album by a Duo or Group and a nomination for Album of the Year. The lead single, "Mientes", received two Latin Grammy awards for Song of the Year and Record of the Year. The album is currently nominated a Lo Nuestro award for "Latin Pop Album of the Year". Mientes was featured in the telenovela Corazón Salvaje (2009-2010) starring Eduardo Yáñez and Aracely Arámbula. Bésame was also featured in the telenovela Triunfo del Amor (2010-2011).

Pneumodesmus newmani is a species of myriapod that lived in the Paleozoic. Its exact age is uncertain; it was originally interpreted as living 428 million years ago, in the Late Silurian; however, subsequent research dates it to around 414 million years old, in the Early Devonian (Lochkovian). It is one of the first myriapods, and among the oldest creatures to have lived on land. It was discovered in 2004, and is known from a single specimen from Stonehaven, Aberdeenshire, Scotland.

Dmitry Tikhonovich Lisanevich (Lang-ru, 1778 – 1825) was a prominent general of Imperial Russian Army. He is best known for negotiation of The Treaty of Kurakchay and killing of Ibrahim Khalil Khan of Karabakh.

Moses Rischin (1925-2020) was an American historian, author, lecturer, editor, and emeritus professor of history at San Francisco State University. He coined the phrase new Mormon history in a 1969 article of the same name.

Rischin is considered an authority on American ethnic and immigration history and a pioneer in the field of American Jewish history. Historian Selma Berrol, however, has challenged the minimal treatment Rischin has given to the tensions between earlier German Jews and later Russian Jews in America.

The Aleutian tern (Onychoprion aleuticus) is a migratory bird living in the subarctic region of the globe most of the year. It is frequently associated with the Arctic tern, which it closely resembles. While both species have a black cap, the Aleutian tern may be distinguished by its white forehead (although juvenile Arctic terns also have white foreheads). During breeding season, the Arctic terns have bright red bills, feet, and legs while those of the Aleutian terns are black.

Onychoprion aleuticus has not been thoroughly studied yet. If its winter migratory range was completely unknown until the late 1980s; it is now known that many Aleutian terns spend the winter near the Equator in the western Pacific. The Aleutian tern breeds in wide-ranging coastal colonies only in Alaska and eastern Siberia. Although Alaskan and Siberian populations are not well monitored, both are thought to be in significant decline.

The Philadelphia Jewish Film Festival (also known as the Gershman Philadelphia Jewish Film Festival (GPJFF)) is an annual film festival in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, in the United States that presents movies and film-related programs about the Jewish experience, culture, values, and legacy.

La Vinia Delois Jennings is an American literary scholar and critic of twentieth-century American literature and culture, currently a Distinguished Humanities Professor at the University of Tennessee, and also formerly a Lindsay Young Professor and a 1998 Fulbright Senior Lecturer appointed to the University of Málaga in Spain.

Kentucky Route 8 is a 114.202-mile-long (183.790 km) east–west state highway divided into two distinct segments across northern Kentucky. The western terminus of the route is at KY 237 near Francisville. The eastern terminus is at U.S. Route 23 in South Portsmouth. The two distinct segments of this route were not meant to be connected together. KY 8 from its west end in Boone County to Augusta in Bracken County is designated as a part of an identified corridor for bikes named the Ramblin' River Tour by the American Association of State Highway and Transportation Officials (AASHTO).

KY 8 is named the Mary Ingles Highway for part of its length. It is rumored that she was the first white woman in Kentucky. Captured by Indians in Virginia in 1755 and taken to Ohio, she later escaped a salt-making party at Big Bone Lick and made her way across the Kentucky wilderness back home to Virginia.

Convoy HX 156 was the 156th of the numbered series of World War II HX convoys of merchant ships from Halifax, Nova Scotia to Liverpool. Forty-three ships departed Halifax on 22 October 1941, and were met two days later by United States Navy Task Unit 4.1.3 consisting of Gleaves-class destroyer Niblack, Clemson-class destroyer Reuben James, Wickes-class destroyer Tarbell, and Benson-class destroyers Benson and Hilary P. Jones.

Dehraadun Diary is a Hindi mystery thriller film released in January 2013. This was the debut film for Ragini Nandwani who made the jump from TV to film industry. The film is based on a real life murder.
The Pearl Carpet of Baroda was a carpet commissioned by the Maharaja of Baroda.

The 2004 New Zealand rugby league season was the 97th season of rugby league that had been played in New Zealand. The main feature of the year was the fifth season of the Bartercard Cup competition that was run by the New Zealand Rugby League. The Mt Albert Lions won the Cup by defeating the Marist Richmond Brothers 40–20 in the Grand Final.
Dolní Bečva is a municipality and village in Vsetín District in the Zlín Region of the Czech Republic. It has about 1,900 inhabitants.
Hans Christian Hagedorn (6 March 1888 – 6 October 1971) was the creator of NPH insulin and the founder of Nordisk Insulinlaboratorium, which is known today as Novo Nordisk.

Susan Jean Silver (born July 17, 1958) is an American music manager, best known for managing Seattle rock bands such as Soundgarden, Alice in Chains and Screaming Trees. Silver also owns the company Susan Silver Management, and co-owns the club The Crocodile in Seattle. Silver was named "the most powerful figure in local rock management" by The Seattle Times in 1991.

First Manhattan Co. (FMC) was founded in 1964 by a group of financial industry executives led by David "Sandy" Gottesman. FMC remains an owner-operated investment advisory firm. Robert Gottesman, David Gottesman's son, is currently the firm's CEO.

FMC is based in New York City.

"Audi" is a song by American rapper Smokepurpp. It was released on May 17, 2017, as the lead single from his debut mixtape Deadstar. The track is considered one of his breakout singles, along with "Ski Mask".
Maureen Sullivan is an American librarian, educator, and organizational consultant who served as the president of the American Library Association from 2012 to 2013.

Woo In-hee (Korean: 우인희, died 1981) was a North Korean actress and a mistress of Kim Jong-il.

Having reached stardom in the 1960s and 1970, Woo, renowned for her beauty, acted in dozens of films. She married Yoo Hosun, a famed film director, but she was involved in affairs outside of her marriage. In the late 1970s, she became a secret mistress of Kim Jong-il. After Woo began an affair with another man, Kim had her publicly executed in front of 6,000 people.

The karbovanets or karbovanet (Lang-uk, plural: карбованці, karbovantsi for 2–4, or карбованців, karbovantsiv for 5 or more), also known as kupon (Lang-uk, plural: купони, kupony) or coupon, has been a distinct unit of currency in Ukraine during three separate periods of the 20th century. It is also a predecessor currency of today's Ukrainian hryvnia.

The karbovanets was subdivided into one hundred kopiykas, but no denominations in kopiykas have ever been issued.

In the ISO 4217 standard, the official name is spelled as karbovanet, while English version of the National Bank of Ukraine's website refers to it as karbovanets.

Marilyn J. Suiter is a geologist whose professional career has spanned teaching, working the oil and gas industry and public services. In her leadership roles at both the American Geosciences Institute and the National Science Foundation, Suiter has worked over decades to increase the ethnic diversity of the geosciences.

Chas Henry (born January 6, 1989) is a former American football punter. Henry played college football for the University of Florida, earned consensus All-American honors, and was recognized as the best college punter as a senior. He was signed by the Philadelphia Eagles as an undrafted free agent in 2011.

Richard Roswell Lyman (November 23, 1870 – December 31, 1963) was an American engineer and religious leader who was an apostle in The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints (LDS Church) from 1918 to 1943.

Lyman is often noted as the most recent LDS Church apostle to have been excommunicated. In 1943, J. Reuben Clark, the first counselor in the LDS church's First Presidency and the church's de facto leader due to church president Heber J. Grant's poor health, became aware that Lyman had for a number of years been in an intimate relationship with a woman he had earlier been assigned to counsel. The Quorum of the Twelve Apostles convened a disciplinary council to hear the case and Lyman's explanation, after which he was immediately excommunicated. Lyman was rebaptized in 1954 at age 83, and his full priesthood blessings were restored posthumously in 1970.

Charles Van Dell Johnson (August 25, 1916 – December 12, 2008) was an American film, television, theatre and radio actor. He was a major star at Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer during and after World War II.

Johnson was described as the embodiment of the "boy-next-door wholesomeness" which made him a popular Hollywood star in the 1940s and 1950s, playing "the red-haired, freckle-faced soldier, sailor, or bomber pilot who used to live down the street" in MGM films during the war years, with such films as Thirty Seconds Over Tokyo, A Guy Named Joe, and The Human Comedy. He made occasional World War II films through the end of the 1960s, and he played a military officer in one of his final feature films in 1992. At the time of his death in 2008, he was one of the last surviving matinee idols of Hollywood's "golden age".
Black Noise: Rap Music and Black Culture in Contemporary America is a 1994 book by Tricia Rose. It was released in hardback on April 29, 1994 through Wesleyan University Press.

RDE-1 (RNAi-DEfective 1) is a primary Argonaute protein required for RNA-mediated interference (RNAi) in Caenorhabditis elegans. The rde-1 gene locus was first characterized in C. elegans mutants resistant to RNAi, and is a member of a highly conserved Piwi gene family that includes plant, Drosophila, and vertebrate homologs.

Mass spectrometry imaging (MSI) is a technique used in mass spectrometry to visualize the spatial distribution of molecules, as biomarkers, metabolites, peptides or proteins by their molecular masses. After collecting a mass spectrum at one spot, the sample is moved to reach another region, and so on, until the entire sample is scanned. By choosing a peak in the resulting spectra that corresponds to the compound of interest, the MS data is used to map its distribution across the sample. This results in pictures of the spatially resolved distribution of a compound pixel by pixel. Each data set contains a veritable gallery of pictures because any peak in each spectrum can be spatially mapped. Despite the fact that MSI has been generally considered a qualitative method, the signal generated by this technique is proportional to the relative abundance of the analyte. Therefore, quantification is possible, when its challenges are overcome. Although widely used traditional methodologies like radiochemistry and immunohistochemistry achieve the same goal as MSI, they are limited in their abilities to analyze multiple samples at once, and can prove to be lacking if researchers do not have prior knowledge of the samples being studied. Most common ionization technologies in the field of MSI are DESI imaging, MALDI imaging and secondary ion mass spectrometry imaging (SIMS imaging).

Mar Emmanuel III Delly (Lang-syr, Lang-ar) (27 September 1927 – 8 April 2014) was the Patriarch Emeritus of Babylon of the Chaldeans and former Primate of the Chaldean Catholic Church, an Eastern Catholic sui juris particular church of the Catholic Church, and also a cardinal. He was born in Tel Keppe and was ordained a priest on 21 December 1952. He was consecrated a bishop in December 1962 at the age of 35. He was elected Patriarch of the Chaldean Church on 3 December 2003, succeeding the late Patriarch Raphael I Bidawid. He was raised to the rank of cardinal bishop on 24 November 2007.
Rikuchū-Noda Station (陸中野田駅, Rikuchū-Noda-eki) is a railway station on the Rias Line in the village of Noda, Iwate, Japan, operated by the third-sector railway operator Sanriku Railway.
Circuit Rider is an album by cornetist/composer Ron Miles with guitarist Bill Frisell and drummer Brian Blade which was released on the Yellowbird label in 2014.

Solder paste is used in the manufacture of printed circuit boards to connect surface mount components to pads on the board. It is also possible to solder through-hole pin in paste components by printing solder paste in and over the holes. The sticky paste temporarily holds components in place; the board is then heated, melting the paste and forming a mechanical bond as well as an electrical connection. The paste is applied to the board by jet printing, stencil printing or syringe; then the components are put in place by a pick-and-place machine or by hand.
Legislative elections were held in Taiwan on 19 December 1992.

Hatton Castle stands on the lower part of Hatton Hill, the most easterly of the Sidlaw Hills, to the south of Newtyle in Angus, Scotland. The castle overlooks the wooded Den of Newtyle, and its views extend across Strathmore and include Ben Lawers and Schiehallion as well as the Angus and Glenshee hills. The 16th-century castle was originally built in a typical Scottish "Z plan" tower house design, as a fortified country house or château. There was an earlier castle called Balcraig Castle which stood less than half a mile from the present building, also on Hatton Hill.

Select Registry, Distinguished Inns of North America, is a non-profit association of more than 300 country inns, B&Bs and boutique hotels throughout the United States. Founded in 1972, the association is based in Greensboro, Georgia, United States and led by CEO Mark Reichle.

Marco Pinotti (born 25 February 1976 in Osio Sotto, Lombardy) is an Italian former road racing cyclist, who competed as a professional between 1999 and 2013. An individual time trial specialist, Pinotti was a six-time Italian Time Trial Champion (2005, 2007, 2008, 2009, 2010 and 2013).

Dick Tracy vs. Crime, Inc. (1941) is a Republic Movie serial based on the Dick Tracy comic strip. It was directed by the team of William Witney and John English with Ralph Byrd reprising his role from the earlier serials. It was the last of the four Dick Tracy serials produced by Republic, although Ralph Byrd went on to portray the character again in two features and on television.

CNN Pipeline was an English language video news service providing both live and on-demand video to subscribers' computers via broadband Internet connections. It was part of the CNN group of news services. The service was subscription-based, and did not contain advertising like other CNN stations. The bandwidth, storage and streaming servers was provided by AOL, which is also owned by Time Warner. Each of the feeds broadcast in the 16:9 aspect ratio, however the resolution of the broadcast prohibited it from being considered a high definition channel, and in many cases not even SDTV.
Callulops fojaensis is a species of frog in the family Microhylidae. It is only known from the Foja mountains in West Papua (Indonesia).
Frederick Eugene Crawford (July 27, 1910 – March 5, 1974) was an American football player.
St. Dominic's Abbey is a medieval Dominican abbey and National Monument located in Cashel, Ireland.

A number of locally-specific units of measurement were used in the Czech lands to measure length, area, capacity and so on. In 1876, the metric system was made compulsory; however, local measurements and old Viennese measurements were still in use.
Phlebiella is a genus of crust fungi in the order Polyporales.

Among the paintings attributed to Giovanni da Rimini (fl. 1292–1336) are two panels from a former diptych, dated to 1300–1305, of which the left wing is in the collection of the National Gallery, London, and the right that of the Galleria Nazionale d'Arte Antica, Palazzo Barberini, Rome.

The Caister Murdoch System (CMS) was a major natural gas collection, processing and transportation system in the UK sector of the southern North Sea. It comprised 11 platforms, 8 subsea wellhead completions and interconnecting pipelines centered about 155 km east of Flamborough Head Yorkshire. It operated from 1993 to 2018.
Paul Alden Brinegar Jr. (December 19, 1917 – March 27, 1995) was an American character actor best known for his roles in three Western series: The Life and Legend of Wyatt Earp, Rawhide, and Lancer.

Lau is a populated area, a socken (not to be confused with parish), on the Swedish island of Gotland. It comprises the same area as the administrative Lau District, established on 1 January 2016. Originally an island, it is now part of the main Gotland island due to the isostasy. It is mostly known for the good water from the spring Lau Käldu.

Michael Joshua Oliverius (born October 10, 1981) is an American former child actor better known by his stage name Michael Oliver.

Oliver is best known for his role as "Junior" in the first two Problem Child movies. He also played Sam Dalton In Dillinger and Capone in 1995.
Ramona Christine Jennex (born June 4, 1955) is a former Canadian educator and politician from the province of Nova Scotia.

The Committee of the Verkhovna Rada of Ukraine on Environmental Policy, Nature Resources Utilization and Elimination of the Consequences of Chornobyl Catastrophe (Lang-uk, Komitet Verkhovnoi Rady Ukrainy z pytan’ ekolohichnoi polityky, pryrodokorystuvannia ta likvidatsii naslidkiv Chornobyl’s’koi katastrofy) is a standing committee of the Verkhovna Rada, Ukraine's unicameral parliament. The committee consists of 15 people's deputies in the parliament's 8th convocation. Its entire composition was approved on December 4, 2014.

Pivotal (19 January 1993 – 19 November 2021) was a British Thoroughbred racehorse and sire. In a racing career restricted to six races between October 1995 and August 1996 he established himself as one of the leading sprinters in Europe. His most important wins came in the King's Stand Stakes and the Nunthorpe Stakes as a three-year-old in the summer of 1996. He was then retired to stud where he became an exceptionally successful breeding stallion.

Akhtarul Wasey (born September 1, 1951) is the president of Maulana Azad University, Jodhpur, India, and a former professor of Islamic Studies. He taught at Jamia Millia Islamia (Central University) in New Delhi, where he remains professor emeritus in the Department of Islamic Studies.
The Puzzle Mansion is a museum in Tagaytay, Cavite, Philippines which hosts a collection of jigsaw puzzle which from 2012 to 2017 was recognized to be the biggest in the world according to Guinness.

The National Center for Health Statistics (NCHS) is a U.S. government agency that provides statistical information to guide actions and policies to improve the public health of the American people. It is a unit of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) and a principal agency of the U.S. Federal Statistical System. It is headquartered at University Town Center in Hyattsville, Maryland, just outside Washington, D.C.
Sthenelaidas (Gr. Σθενελαίδας) was a Spartan who held the office of ephor in 432 BC, during which he played a critical role in starting the Peloponnesian War against Athens.

Formula One 99 is a racing video game developed by Studio 33 and published by Psygnosis for PlayStation and Microsoft Windows. It is the sequel to the 1998 video game Formula 1 98 and was based on the 1999 Formula One World Championship.

Following the disappointment of Formula 1 98, and the subsequent split with Visual Science, Psygnosis hired Studio 33 to develop Formula One 99, after their successful development of Newman/Haas Racing the previous year. During production of the game, Sony completed a full acquisition of Psygnosis, making this the final Formula One game to be published under the Psygnosis name.

The game is unique in having substitute drivers appear in the game as they did in the real 1999 season (such as Mika Salo replacing Michael Schumacher for the races between Austria and Europe). A new grid editor tool was also introduced, allowing players to customise the starting grid to their own liking before a race. Despite the lack of an arcade mode that had featured in previous titles, the game was widely praised as an overwhelming improvement in comparison to the 1998 game.
Zohra Begum Kazi (15 October 1912 – 7 November 2007) was the first Bengali Muslim female physician. She was awarded Tamgha-e-Pakistan (1964), Begum Rokeya Padak (2002) and Ekushey Padak (2008).

Going Blank Again is the second studio album by English rock band Ride, released on 9 March 1992 on Creation Records. It was produced by Alan Moulder, and peaked at No. 5 in the UK Albums Chart. In October 2009 the album was certified gold by the British Phonographic Industry for sales of over 100,000 units.

Mystic Comics is the name of three comic book series published by the company that eventually became Marvel Comics. The first two series were superhero anthologies published by Marvel's 1930-1940s predecessor, Timely Comics, during what fans and historians call the Golden Age of comic books. The third, simply titled Mystic, was a horror fiction-suspense anthology from Marvel's 1950s forerunner, Atlas Comics.

Mijar is a village in the Manglore taluk in the city of Mangaluru of Dakshina Kannada district of the Indian state of Karnataka. Tenkamijaru Grama Panchayat which is 30 km from Mangalore, 12 km from the famous pilgrimage site Jain Kashi. The Grama Panchayat Contains 2 Revenue Villages, cultivation as the main source of income, the level of education and the quality of life of the people are in general. Gram Panchayat consists of Scheduled Castes, Scheduled Tribes, Backward Classes and Others.Hindu Muslim Christian, Jains lead lives in harmony.

The village has a total of 7 wards and has 19 members, Anganwadi Primary School, High School and PU College are located in the Gram Panchayat area. Includes 2 Engineering Colleges and MBA Courses. Ayurvedic herbal preparation parmesis, operating cashew factory, are useful in improving the quality of life of people.The Panchayath has its own fixed and variable assets and is always responsive to people's problems

There are mosques and temples in the Panchayath area, The temple of Lord Vishnu and Somanatheshwara the village headman The Dargah of Muslims in Thodar is religiously famous, People are living in communion with social politics and religion.

Velocity Tower is a mixed use development next to the Inner Ring Road in Sheffield City Centre, South Yorkshire, England, overlooking Ecclesall Road. The build is a continuation of a previous design to be built by Cala, titled 'Eclipse'.

The bullace is a variety of plum. It bears edible fruit similar to those of the damson, and like the damson is considered to be a strain of the insititia subspecies of Prunus domestica. Although the term has regionally been applied to several different kinds of "wild plum" found in the United Kingdom, it is usually taken to refer to varieties with a spherical shape, as opposed to the oval damsons.

Unlike nearly all damsons, bullaces may be either "white" (i.e. yellow or green) or "black" (i.e. blue or purple) in colour, and ripen up to six weeks later in the year. Though smaller than most damsons, bullaces are much larger than the closely related sloe. Their flavour is usually rather acidic until fully ripe.

Sea of Love is the third studio album by South Korean R&B duo Fly to the Sky. It was released via SM Entertainment on April 26, 2022. Commercially, the album peaked at number two on the monthly RIAK album chart and sold almost 250,000 copies by the end of the year.

San José Castle -also known as Castillo de San José in Spanish- was opened to the public on 25 May 1846 on the «Buena Vista Hill» on the southeast of Guatemala City by general Rafael Carrera government; it was also known as «Carrera Castle». San José Fort was located where later Guatemalan administrations built a new city Hall, a National Theater and part of the Bolivar Avenue. The fort had a shooting range for the soldiers, ammunition warehouses, horses, diners and a small pond that was called "Soldier's Pond".

Cécile Rol-Tanguy (10 April 1919 – 8 May 2020) was a French communist who was a Resistance fighter during World War II. She participated in the liberation of Paris serving as secretariat and a liaison officer, conducting clandestine operations and relaying confidential communications.
Henry-Louis de La Grange (26 May 1924 – 27 January 2017) was a French musicologist and biographer of Gustav Mahler.
The 1994–95 Atlante F.C. season was the 4th season since the team's last promotion to Primera División. Atlante competed in Primera División and Copa México

Potamia (Greek: Ποταμιά, Turkish: Potamya, Bodamya and Dereliköy) is a village in the north-east of Cyprus in the district of Nicosia, close to the Green Line separating it from the area of the breakaway, unrecognised Northern Cyprus. Together with Pyla and Rizokarpaso, Potamia forms one of the few remaining ethnically mixed communities in Cyprus, made up of Greek and Turkish Cypriots.

The 1991 State of Origin series saw the tenth time the annual three-match State of Origin series between the New South Wales and Queensland representative rugby league teams was played entirely under 'state of origin' selection rules. It was notable as Wally Lewis'farewell from Origin football and featured his half-time stoush with Mark Geyer in Game II which match culminated in Michael O'Connor's sensational match-winning sideline conversion in teeming rain.

For the first time since State of Origin became a three-game series in 1982, Queensland named the same starting XIII for each game of the series. The Maroons only made two changes during the series, with Origin veterans Dale Shearer coming onto the bench for Game 2 in Sydney to replace Steve Renouf, and Bob Lindner, the player of the 1990 Kangaroo tour, named on the bench for Game 3 in Brisbane (replacing Gavin Allen) after recovering from a broken leg. This saw Queensland only use 19 players for the series. In contrast, NSW only had seven players there for the entire series (five of whom started each game) and used a total of 24 players.

Acetoin, also known as 3-hydroxybutanone or acetyl methyl carbinol, is an organic compound with the formula CH3CH(OH)C(O)CH3. It is a colorless liquid with a pleasant, buttery odor. It is chiral. The form produced by bacteria is (R)-acetoin.

The CMM 400-2 is a diesel multiple unit produced by Materfer in Córdoba Province, Argentina. The units are produced in Argentina's three primary track gauges and the standard gauge variants were the first to be produced for General Urquiza Railway services in Entre Ríos Province.

Advance America (formerly Citizens Concerned for the Constitution) is a conservative political advocacy group in the U.S. state of Indiana. Advance America claims that it is a "non-partisan tax exempt, educational organization." It claims affiliation with approximately 4,000 Indiana churches, nearly one third of all churches in the state of Indiana.

Steinfurt-Burgsteinfurt station is the main station of the town of Steinfurt in western Munsterland in the German state of North Rhine-Westphalia and is located in the district of Burgsteinfurt. The station is a former railway junction on the Münster–Enschede railway. The Coesfeld–Rheine railway and the Borken–Steinfurt railway are closed and largely dismantled.
56 Field Regiment (Jitra) is part of the Regiment of Artillery of the Indian Army.

Zheng Zhi (Chinese: 郑智; pinyin: Zhèng Zhì; born 20 August 1980) is a Chinese professional footballer and manager who played most of his career for Chinese Super League club Guangzhou F.C., becoming their captain and serving also as their caretaker manager in two stints. After starting his career as a defender, Zheng was later moved into a central midfield role by then head coach Zhu Guanghu at Shenzhen Jianlibao and experienced immediate success there by winning the 2004 league title with the club. A move to Shandong Luneng Taishan saw a prolific goal scoring period in his career and he soon became the captain of the Chinese national team, which then led to moves to Charlton Athletic and Celtic. He moved back to China in 2010 and joined Guangzhou Evergrande, making over 300 appearances so far as captain while helping the club win all major trophies a Chinese club could compete for, including Chinese Super League for a record 8 times and AFC Champions League twice.

Bugando Medical Centre (BMC) is a tertiary care medical facility owned by the Episcopal Conference of the Catholic Bishops of Tanzania. The hospital is operated in collaboration with the Tanzania Ministry of Health and Social Welfare.

Pieter Woortman (born 23 March 1700 – 14 April 1780) was a slave trader and an administrator of the Dutch West India Company. He was one of the longest-serving Director-General of the Dutch Gold Coast, in office between 1767 and 1769 (ad interim) and from 1769 until his death in 1780.

Benedicto “Bong” Ducat (born May 6, 1957) is a Filipino impressionist painter. He is now teaching at the University of Santo Tomas College of Fine Arts and Design. He is noted for his renderings of plants, flowers and nature in droplet-like brush strokes and bright colors. In college, Ducat won the University of Santo Tomas Benavides Civic Award.

Jeremy Maurice Jacobs Sr. (born January 21, 1940) is an American billionaire businessman, the owner of the Boston Bruins and chairman of Delaware North. Forbes magazine ranks him as 481st richest person in the world.

American International School of Abuja (AISA) is a coeducational international day school in Abuja, Nigeria serving grades Pre-School through Grade 12.

The school year runs from August to June, with 180 student contact days. At the end of the 2015–2016 school year, the student population was 470 spanning from over 40 countries.

"The Things That I Used to Do" is a blues standard written by Guitar Slim. He recorded it in New Orleans, where the young Ray Charles arranged and produced the session. Specialty Records released the song as a single in 1953 and it became a bestseller the following year. Specialty founder Art Rupe believed that the appeal would be limited to the Southern U.S. rural audience. However, urban rhythm and blues radio stations in the North began airing the song and built it into a national hit. As a result, Guitar Slim became in great demand as a performer and played at venues such as the Apollo Theater in New York City.

The single was one of the biggest hits in Specialty's history and stayed on the Billboard's Rhythm and Blues Records charts for 42 weeks. The song remained at number one for six weeks and was the best-selling R&B record of the year, selling more than a million copies.
Lunella undulata, common name the common warrener or the lightning turban, is a species of sea snail, marine gastropod mollusk in the family Turbinidae.
